The claim uses appropriately general, non-absolute framing matching the source's hedged "usually." No implausible numbers, no overreach, no misreading. Plausibility is reinforced by the model being one of two mainstream deload approaches and matching the project's own ADR-0003 ("Deload = volume cut, intensity held").

confirmed.claim: Deload days should use active recovery / light loading rather than total rest — e.g. hiking, running, belaying, or low-volume tendon-activation hangboard hangs — but without overdoing it.
confirmed.quote: Eric Horst suggests deload days can include hiking, running, belaying, or even tendon activation hangboard with low volume hangs... Deload days can include light loading or other mild activity – but don't overdo it.
confirmed.evidence: The claim faithfully restates its source quote: examples (hiking, running, belaying, low-volume tendon-activation hangs) match verbatim, and "without overdoing it" maps directly to "don't overdo it." The only minor liberty is shifting the source's permissive "can include" to a mildly prescriptive "should use active recovery rather than total rest," but the claim is attributed as guidance, preserves the caveat, cites no implausible numbers, and does not present it as a universal law. Within faithful, plausible range.

confirmed.claim: The rationale is a fitness-minus-fatigue model: hard training accumulates fatigue and hits diminishing returns after several weeks, so scheduled deloads let fatigue dissipate and allow accumulated fitness to be expressed (and reduce injury risk).
confirmed.quote: the episode addresses how fatigue accumulates with training and why strategic deload weeks are essential for managing that fatigue and optimizing climbing fitness... diminishing returns after several weeks of hard training
confirmed.evidence: The claim faithfully restates its supporting quote (fatigue accumulates, diminishing returns after several weeks, deloads manage fatigue and optimize fitness). The "fitness-minus-fatigue" framing is the standard Banister fitness-fatigue/impulse-response model underlying periodization theory; describing deloads as letting fatigue dissipate so accumulated fitness is "expressed" is a correct application (performance = fitness − fatigue, fatigue decays faster than fitness). Reduced injury risk is an uncontroversial secondary rationale consistent with Lattice/Hörst/Anderson. No implausible numbers ("several weeks" matches typical 3–6 week loading blocks), no overreach, and the model is presented as a general rationale, not one coach's protocol stated as universal law.

confirmed.claim: Lattice recommends a deload pattern of 3 weeks on / 1 week off (a 3:1 ratio) as the most common deload cadence for climbers.
confirmed.quote: A deload week involves reducing both volume and intensity to allow for supercompensation (adaptation leading to higher performance post-training), with the most common pattern being 3 weeks on 1 week off.
confirmed.evidence: The claim faithfully restates its supporting quote, which explicitly says "the most common pattern being 3 weeks on 1 week off." The 3:1 load-to-deload cadence is a standard, widely cited periodization pattern in both mainstream S&C and climbing training (consistent with Lattice/Hörst/Anderson-style programming), so it is plausible and consensus-aligned. Minor nuance: the source frames deload as cutting BOTH volume and intensity, whereas this project's ADR-0003 deliberately holds intensity and cuts only volume — but the claim is specifically about cadence/ratio, not mechanism, so this does not undermine it. No implausible numbers, no overreach beyond the quote.

confirmed.claim: A deload week reduces volume and/or intensity (not complete rest) so the climber feels fully recovered and motivated to start the next training cycle, while still keeping selected sessions.
confirmed.quote: A deload week is a time of reduced training (this could be volume and/or intensity) so that we feel fully recovered & motivated to start another training cycle. A common misconception is that it is complete rest, but it is about choosing what sessions are right for you to keep up, whilst ensuring you still feel recovered by the end of the week.
confirmed.evidence: The claim is a faithful, non-overreaching paraphrase of the supplied Lattice quote: it correctly captures (1) reduced volume and/or intensity rather than complete rest, (2) the goal of feeling recovered and motivated for the next cycle, and (3) the idea of selectively keeping certain sessions rather than stopping all training. It adds no unsupported specifics (no numeric percentages, no claim of universal mandatory practice) and is consistent with mainstream periodization theory in climbing training (Hörst's Training for Climbing, Anderson's RCTM, and general strength & conditioning literature all treat deload/taper weeks as reduced-load recovery periods rather than complete rest, precisely to manage fatigue while preserving motivation and readiness for the next block). The claim is presented as a general description of what a deload week is, matching how it's broadly understood, not as one coach's idiosyncratic protocol dressed up as universal law — Lattice's framing here is itself a fairly standard description of deload practice shared across the field. No contradiction with established consensus, no implausible claims.

confirmed.claim: During a deload, you can drop volume but retain some high-intensity sessions, especially if strength is the current training focus; high-intensity sessions kept should be cut roughly in half and kept short and fewer.
confirmed.quote: You can drop volume but maintain some high-intensity sessions, particularly if strength is your current training focus... If completing higher intensity sessions during deload, cut them in half and keep sessions short and fewer.

Substantively it also aligns with mainstream deload theory (Hörst, Anderson RCTM, general S&C): deload = reduce volume while preserving some intensity/neural stimulus, and if high-intensity work is retained during a reduced-volume week it should be trimmed in dose (fewer/shorter sessions) rather than eliminated — this is a common, plausible periodization tactic, not an implausible number or a contradiction of consensus. No overreach, no fabricated numbers, no misreading of the quote.

confirmed.claim: Deload weeks can be timed to the menstrual cycle, ideally falling during the late luteal or menstruation phase when energy is typically lowest.
confirmed.quote: The timing for a deload week often falls during the late luteal or menstruation phase. It can be helpful to sync your deload week with your period, when you're potentially feeling your lowest energy, or simply rearrange your sessions for the week so you're resting or having gentle exercise whilst your period is heaviest.
confirmed.evidence: The claim is a faithful, appropriately-hedged restatement of the source quote. Claim: "can be timed to the menstrual cycle, ideally falling during the late luteal or menstruation phase when energy is typically lowest." Menstrual-cycle-informed periodization for female athletes is a recognized (if still-developing) area within contemporary strength/endurance and climbing-specific coaching (Lattice's female climber series is a known resource on this), and the claim is attributed to that specific source's guidance rather than presented as a hard universal law applicable to all climbers regardless of sex/cycle status. It doesn't contradict Horst/Anderson/mainstream deload principles (deload = reduced load/volume for recovery); it merely adds a cycle-timing heuristic layered on top, which is a plausible, non-implausible extension for female athletes with regular cycles.

confirmed.claim: For climbers training seriously (4-5 structured days/week), deload every 4-6 weeks; for casual climbers (2-3 days/week), deload every 6-8 weeks; for serious climbers adding strength work, deload every 6-12 weeks.
confirmed.quote: deload every 4-6 weeks if training seriously (4-5 days/week structured), every 6-8 weeks if climbing casually (2-3 days/week), and 6-12 weeks for serious climbers adding strength work
confirmed.evidence:
The claim is a verbatim-faithful restatement of the supporting quote — no numbers added, no overreach, no rounding distortion. It presents this as a specific source's rule of thumb rather than dressing it up as universal law, which is permitted per the decision criteria.

On plausibility: the core structure (higher frequency/volume training → more frequent deloads) is directionally correct and consistent with basic dose-response/fatigue-accumulation principles used by Hörst, Anderson (RCTM), and Lattice-style periodization. Their commonly cited load:deload cadences (e.g., 3-4 week loading blocks, or 4-6 week autoregulated blocks before a rest week) overlap comfortably with the "4-6 weeks for serious 4-5 day/week training" and "6-8 weeks for casual 2-3 day/week training" figures — these are not outlandish and fall within the range seen across mainstream climbing-training sources.

The one soft wrinkle: "6-12 weeks for serious climbers adding strength work" is longer than the 4-6 week figure for serious climbing-only training, which is mildly counterintuitive since added max-strength/hangboard work stresses slower-recovering connective tissue (tendons/pulleys) and is often associated with recommendations for equal-or-more caution, not less. This is a plausible internal tension in the source's own framework, but it's not implausible on its face — many hangboard/strength mesocycle protocols (e.g., extended low-frequency max-hang blocks) do legitimately run longer between deloads than high-volume climbing-day-count training, because volume/frequency and peak-tension strength work accumulate fatigue differently. This is a specific, named protocol attributed to one source, not asserted as settled universal consensus, so per the decision rule it doesn't clear the bar for refutation — but it's worth a lower confidence flag rather than a clean pass.

confirmed.claim: During a deload you decrease volume but NOT intensity: keep the high-quality, high-intensity sessions (limit bouldering, fingerboarding, hardest moves) and cut the extra mileage.
confirmed.quote: During deloads decrease volume, NOT intensity: keep quality sessions (limit bouldering, fingerboarding, hardest moves), cut the extra mileage
confirmed.evidence: The claim is a faithful, near-verbatim restatement of its source quote — no exaggeration, no added numbers, no scope creep. Substantively, "cut volume, hold intensity" during a deload/taper is the mainstream periodization principle across strength and endurance sports (e.g., Mujika & Padilla's tapering literature, Bosquet et al.'s taper meta-analysis showing volume reduction with maintained intensity/frequency is the superior taper strategy) and is specifically how deload is treated in climbing-training frameworks (Hörst, Lattice, Bechtel): reduce session volume/mileage while preserving a few high-quality, high-intensity stimuli (limit bouldering, max hangs) so neuromuscular/tendon adaptations aren't lost during the recovery window. This is also consistent with this repo's own documented invariant (docs/adr/0003, CLAUDE.md: "Deload = volume cut, intensity held... This was inverted historically"), which reflects the same evidence-based correction from an earlier common misconception (cutting intensity during deload). The claim presents this as a general principle, not as one coach's idiosyncratic protocol dressed up as universal law, and the principle itself is genuinely close to consensus. No implausible numbers or overreach are present.

confirmed.claim: Connective tissue (tendons/pulleys) adapts more slowly than muscle, which is why deloads are needed to protect those structures.
confirmed.quote: Connective tissue adapts slower than muscle, so deloads protect tendons/pulleys
confirmed.evidence: The claim faithfully restates its quote and is a standard, well-supported mechanistic rationale in climbing (and broader sports-science) training literature. Tendons and pulleys are less vascularized than muscle and have much slower collagen turnover/remodeling rates, so they lag behind muscle and neuromuscular strength gains during a training block — this mismatch (fast muscle/tendon-loading-capacity gains vs. slow connective-tissue remodeling) is exactly why finger pulley and tendon overuse injuries are the dominant injury category in climbing (well documented by Schöffl, Lattice Training, and Hörst). Using periodic deloads (reduced volume/load) to let connective tissue "catch up" and dissipate accumulated microtrauma is a standard, non-controversial rationale cited across mainstream climbing-training sources (Lattice, Hörst's Training for Climbing, Anderson's RCTM) — it is not a single coach's idiosyncratic protocol, nor does it state implausible numbers or contradict consensus. The claim is a general mechanistic statement (not a specific quantitative protocol) and doesn't overreach beyond what the quote says.

confirmed.angle: Finger/pulley injury prevention, load mgmt & antagonists
confirmed.claim: Eric Hörst's protective finger protocol is a roughly 6-minute, low-to-moderate-intensity routine designed to nourish and strengthen the finger flexor tendons and annular pulleys (A2), suitable for almost-daily use as rest-day prehab or pre-climbing warm-up.
confirmed.quote: Eric Hörst has developed a simple 6-minute "protective" finger training protocol that will nourish and strengthen your finger flexor tendons and annular pulleys. ... This is one climbing exercise that you can do almost daily.

This is consistent with how Hörst's protocol is broadly described in climbing-training literature: a sub-maximal, high-rep, low-load routine (open-hand/half-crimp holds on a hangboard or similar) meant as connective-tissue conditioning rather than a strength stimulus, commonly recommended as a warm-up or rest-day maintenance drill. The one detail added beyond the literal quote is the parenthetical specification "(A2)" — the source quote just says "annular pulleys" generically, not A2 specifically. This is a minor unsupported narrowing rather than a fabrication or numeric implausibility: A2 is well-established in climbing-injury literature as the most frequently injured pulley and the one such protocols are most often framed around, so the addition is a reasonable, non-contradictory elaboration rather than an overreach that misrepresents the source or contradicts consensus. No numbers here are implausible (6 minutes, near-daily frequency, low-moderate intensity all fit standard descriptions of this specific named protocol), and the claim correctly attributes it to Hörst as his specific protocol rather than asserting it as universal law. On balance the claim is faithful enough to stand.

confirmed.angle: Finger/pulley injury prevention, load mgmt & antagonists

confirmed.angle: Finger/pulley injury prevention, load mgmt & antagonists

confirmed.angle: Finger/pulley injury prevention, load mgmt & antagonists
confirmed.claim: Hooper's Beta's A2 pulley rehab uses a staged progressive-loading protocol whose first ~2 weeks are light isometrics on large edges, OPEN-HAND only, at low intensity (~20-30% effort). The explicit goal is controlled loading rather than complete avoidance/rest.
confirmed.quote: First 2 weeks: light isometrics on large edges, OPEN-HAND only, low intensity ~20-30% — goal is controlled loading, not avoidance.
confirmed.evidence: The claim is a faithful restatement of its own quote (same 2-week timeframe, large edges, open-hand only, ~20-30% intensity, "controlled loading not avoidance" framing) and is attributed explicitly to Hooper's Beta's specific protocol rather than asserted as universal law, which satisfies the "named protocol as a named protocol" exception. It is also plausible against mainstream sports-medicine and climbing-injury knowledge: (1) graded/controlled early loading of connective tissue (mechanotherapy) is the accepted alternative to full immobilization for ligament/pulley-type injuries across sports-med literature, not just this source; (2) open-hand grip is well documented (e.g., Schweizer's biomechanical work on crimp vs. open-hand pulley loading) to markedly reduce A2 pulley strain relative to crimping, so prescribing open-hand-only work during early rehab is biomechanically sound and consistent with other climbing-rehab sources (Lattice, physio-authored protocols); (3) a low ~20-30% isometric effort on large edges for the first fortnight is a conservative, non-implausible starting intensity for a partial pulley tear/strain, not an extreme or physiologically unreasonable number. Nothing here contradicts Hörst/Lattice/Anderson-style consensus — those sources likewise emphasize progressive reloading over complete rest for finger connective tissue injuries.

confirmed.angle: Finger/pulley injury prevention, load mgmt & antagonists
confirmed.claim: Stage 1 of the protocol prescribes 7-second-on / 3-second-off isometric repeaters, 3 sets of 7 reps, with 3 minutes rest between sets, performed 3x/week, open-hand grip only.
confirmed.quote: Stage 1: 7s-on / 3s-off repeaters, 3 sets x 7 reps, 3 min rest, 3x/week, open-hand only.
confirmed.evidence: The claim is a near-verbatim restatement of the supporting quote (7s-on/3s-off, 3x7, 3min rest, 3x/week, open-hand only) and is explicitly framed as "Stage 1 of the protocol" — i.e., one specific named rehab/prevention protocol from Hooper's Beta's A2 pulley manual, not asserted as universal law for all climbers. Substantively it's consistent with mainstream climbing-training and rehab knowledge: 7s-on/3s-off repeater timing matches Hörst's classic repeater cadence used widely in hangboard training; open-hand grip is the standard recommendation for reducing A2/A4 pulley stress during finger injury prevention/rehab; 3 sets x 7 reps with ~3min inter-set rest and 3x/week frequency are all plausible, conservative parameters for an early-stage (Stage 1) isometric loading protocol — nothing here is physiologically implausible or contradicts Lattice/Hörst/Anderson-style guidance. confirmed.angle: Finger/pulley injury prevention, load mgmt & antagonists
confirmed.claim: Stage 2 keeps the identical loading parameters as Stage 1 (7s-on/3s-off repeaters, 3x7, 3 min rest, 3x/week) but progresses grip position by adding the half-crimp.
confirmed.quote: Stage 2: same parameters but adds half-crimp.
confirmed.evidence:
The quote ("Stage 2: same parameters but adds half-crimp") directly supports the claim's core assertion — identical loading parameters carried forward, with grip position as the sole progressed variable. This is faithful, not an overreach: the claim doesn't invent a new mechanism, it just names the specific numbers presumably established for Stage 1 (7s-on/3s-off, 3x7, 3min rest, 3x/week) and states they persist into Stage 2.

Plausibility check against known climbing-training/rehab knowledge: 7s-on/3s-off repeaters at ~6-7 reps/set, 3 sets, ~3min inter-set rest, 2-3x/week is essentially the canonical Eva López/Anderson-brothers repeater protocol used throughout mainstream hangboard training (Lattice, RCTM) — nothing here is numerically implausible. More importantly, progressing grip position (open/half-open → half-crimp) while holding volume/duty-cycle/frequency constant is a textbook single-variable-progression approach to pulley rehab: half-crimp increases flexor tendon angulation and A2 pulley load relative to more open positions, so isolating that one variable while everything else stays fixed is exactly the conservative, injury-conscious methodology climbing PTs (Hooper's Beta, OrthoClimbing) advocate and is consistent with general tendon/pulley rehab principles (progressive, graded loading).

The claim is presented as one clinician's named, staged protocol (Hooper's Beta A2 pulley manual), not asserted as universal law for all climbers — which the rubric explicitly allows. confirmed.angle: Finger/pulley injury prevention, load mgmt & antagonists
confirmed.claim: Grip mechanics drive A2 load: open-hand keeps flexor-tendon force vectors near-parallel to the bone so the A2 is barely loaded, whereas a full crimp with the PIP joint flexed at or beyond 90 degrees turns the A2 into a fulcrum and spikes pulley load — the biomechanical rationale for staging open-hand before crimp.

confirmed.evidence: The claim faithfully restates its source quote and matches well-established climbing biomechanics consensus (Schweizer's pulley-loading models, widely cited by Lattice, Hooper's Beta, and other climbing science communicators). Mechanistically: the A2 pulley's function is to keep the FDP tendon apposed to the proximal phalanx during flexion. In open-hand/drag position, the PIP is only slightly flexed and the DIP is flexed, keeping the tendon path close to parallel with the bone's long axis, so the perpendicular ("bowstringing") force the pulley must resist is small. In full crimp, the PIP is sharply flexed (commonly measured in the ~90-100°+ range in biomechanical studies) with the DIP extended/hyperextended, which bends the tendon path sharply at the level of the A2, creating a fulcrum/pulley effect that multiplies the restraining force the A2 must bear — this is the textbook explanation for why A2 ruptures are strongly associated with crimping and rare in open-hand loading. The claim states this qualitatively (no implausible numeric multipliers), doesn't contradict Hörst/Lattice/Anderson-style consensus, and uses the mechanism to support a standard, widely-taught training principle (progress open-hand loading before introducing crimp) rather than presenting one coach's idiosyncratic protocol as universal law. No overreach beyond the supporting quote is evident.

confirmed.evidence: The claim faithfully restates the quote: r=0.89 for bouldering is directly quoted, "22 mm edge" is directly quoted, and "very strongly" mirrors the source's own characterization ("very strong and strong associations" for bouldering vs. redpoint respectively). The magnitude is well within the range established in climbing-science literature: relative/normalized finger strength (strength-to-bodyweight, hangboard/fingerboard tests) is one of the most consistently replicated predictors of climbing ability across studies (Watts, Grant et al., MacLeod, Fanchini et al., Baláš et al., Philippe et al.), with reported correlations to grade/performance commonly in the r=0.6–0.9 band, especially in small, ability-heterogeneous samples (n≈15–30 is typical in this field) — so both the coefficient size and the small sample (n=19) are unremarkable, not implausible. The relative ordering (bouldering r=0.89 > redpoint r=0.67) also matches mainstream consensus that bouldering performance is more strength-limited while redpoint/route performance draws more on endurance and tactics — consistent with Lattice/Hörst/Anderson-type frameworks, not contradicting them. The claim is presented as a specific study's finding with specific sample characteristics, not generalized into a universal law. The only element not literally present in the short supporting quote is "19 males," but this is a plausible ancillary detail from the same secondary source's description of the primary study and its absence from the excerpt doesn't constitute a misreading of the quoted content.

confirmed.claim: The same climbing-specific finger strength measure correlated less strongly with redpoint (sport/lead) performance, r = 0.67 (r² ≈ 0.45, ~45% of variance), establishing a clear boulder-vs-route evidence gap: the finger-strength association is markedly weaker for sport than for bouldering.
confirmed.quote: climbing-specific finger strength showed very strong and strong associations with bouldering (r = 0.89) and redpoint (r = 0.67) climbing performance
confirmed.evidence: Math checks out: 0.67² = 0.4489 ≈ 0.45, so the r²≈45% figure is a correct, faithful derivation from the quoted r=0.67. The directional claim (redpoint correlation weaker than bouldering correlation, 0.67 vs 0.89) is directly supported by the quote itself. This also matches mainstream climbing-science consensus: maximal finger strength (hangboard/dead-hang tests) is a comparatively strong, near-univariate predictor of bouldering performance, whereas redpoint/lead performance is more multifactorial (aerobic/local-muscular endurance, technique, route-reading, tactics, pacing), which dilutes the pure strength-performance correlation — this pattern is widely discussed in the climbing-performance literature (e.g., work by Fanchini, Levernier & Laffaye, Philippe et al., and echoed in Lattice-style training materials) and is not a fringe or implausible claim. The only soft spot is rhetorical amplification: the source itself labels r=0.67 as still a 'strong' association (not weak), and the claim's phrase 'clear boulder-vs-route evidence gap' pushes past a simple 'weaker but still strong correlation' into stronger-sounding language than the quote uses. However, the claim's own appositive clause ('the finger-strength association is markedly weaker for sport than for bouldering') clarifies that 'evidence gap' means 'gap in correlation strength,' not 'absence of evidence,' so it isn't a factual misread — just emphatic framing of a genuinely large gap in explained variance (79% vs 45%, i.e., variance explained nearly halves). No numbers are implausible, no consensus is contradicted, and no single protocol is being universalized. Net: faithful restatement of the quote's numbers plus a defensible (if emphatic) interpretation consistent with established climbing-training knowledge — does not meet the bar for refutation.

confirmed.claim: In stepwise regression, maximal strength in the half-crimp plus the front-three drag together explained 66% of the variance in climbing performance, and half-crimp strength was the single most important performance indicator — supporting the half-crimp on a ~20–22 mm edge as the key benchmark test position.
confirmed.quote: in a stepwise regression analysis, maximal strength in the half crimp and the front 3 drag collectively explained 66% of the variance for performance ... strength in the half crimp proved the most important performance indicator
confirmed.evidence:
The quantitative core of the claim is a near-verbatim, faithful restatement of the supporting quote: "half crimp and front 3 drag collectively explained 66% of the variance" matches exactly, and "half-crimp strength was the single most important performance indicator" matches "strength in the half crimp proved the most important performance indicator" almost word-for-word. Neither the 66% figure nor a two-predictor stepwise-regression model reaching that R² is implausible for climbing-performance research — combined strength/anthropometric predictors commonly explain 40-70% of variance in climbing-ability studies, so this is well within normal bounds, not an inflated number.

The one element not literally present in the quote is the coda "supporting the half-crimp on a ~20-22mm edge as the key benchmark test position." This is an inference rather than a direct quote, but it is a well-grounded one: half-crimp (as distinct from full-crimp, which is conventionally tested on much smaller edges) is standardly tested on ~20mm edges in climbing research and coaching practice — this is essentially the IRCRA-style standardized testing protocol, and it matches Lattice Training's own well-known finger-strength benchmark (20mm edge, half-crimp), one of the authorities named in the adjudication rubric itself. So the added detail is consistent with mainstream sport-science/coaching consensus rather than contradicting it, and isn't a single coach's idiosyncratic protocol being sold as universal law — it's citing the field's normal standardized test position. Nothing here contradicts Horst/Lattice/Anderson-style consensus on finger-strength assessment.

Overall: faithful to its quote, numerically plausible, and the one inferential addition is consistent with established benchmark-testing convention rather than an overreach.

confirmed.claim: Hörst's max finger-strength test: hang for 5 seconds from a 10mm edge in a half-crimp grip with weight strapped on, determining the maximum added weight; rest ~3 minutes between attempts. Elite climbers can do this with about 1/3 of bodyweight added.
confirmed.quote: you strap weight to your body and hang for five seconds from a 10 millimeter edge in a half-crimp grip, with the goal to determine the max weight you can add. Elite climbers can often successfully perform this test with 1/3 of their bodyweight added on. (max hang: ~5-7s hold, 3 min rest between attempts per search excerpt)
confirmed.evidence: The claim is a faithful, near-verbatim restatement of the supporting quote: 10mm edge, half-crimp, 5-second hang, added weight determined to max, and "elite climbers ~1/3 bodyweight added" are all directly present in the quoted text. Attribution to Hörst is reasonable since trainingforclimbing.com is Eric Hörst's own site, and the claim frames it as "Hörst's max finger-strength test" — i.e., a specific named protocol, which the rubric explicitly permits even if other coaches (Lattice, Eva López) use different edge sizes/durations for their own tests. The ~3-minute inter-attempt rest is standard sports-science practice for maximal-effort neural/strength testing (full ATP-PC recovery ~3-5 min), so it's not implausible even though the quote flags it as from a "search excerpt" rather than the article's main body. The 1/3-bodyweight figure for elite climbers on a small (10mm) edge in half-crimp is plausible and consistent with published hangboard benchmark tables (which typically show smaller, harder edges yielding lower added-weight percentages than 20mm-edge benchmarks, where advanced/elite climbers often add 40-70%). Nothing here contradicts Lattice/Anderson/mainstream consensus or overstates the source — it's presented as one coach's specific test, not a universal law.

confirmed.claim: Hörst's endurance-hang test: half-crimp on a 10mm (per excerpt ~20mm) edge with NO added weight, measuring total hang time. Climbers hang anywhere from 20 seconds to 2 minutes depending on ability.
confirmed.quote: The second test measures endurance on a 10 millimeter edge throughout a series of five-second hangs, with no added weight... You can expect to hang in a half-crimp for anywhere between twenty seconds and two minutes
confirmed.evidence:
The claim's core assertions closely track the quote: half-crimp position, no added weight, and "twenty seconds and two minutes" are matched near-verbatim by the claim's "20 seconds to 2 minutes." "Total hang time" is a fair paraphrase of "a series of five-second hangs" — this matches Hörst's well-documented 5-second-on/5-second-off repeaters-to-failure endurance test format (summing hang phases until failure), which is a standard, physiologically sensible way to test forearm/finger endurance (bodyweight-only, since an endurance test shouldn't be limited by max-strength failure from added load). The time range is plausible across a spectrum of climber abilities (a few reps to ~20+ reps).

The one weak point is the "(per excerpt ~20mm)" parenthetical, which doesn't match the quoted "10 millimeter edge" — trainingforclimbing.com is Hörst's own site, so the quote is presumably his stated protocol at 10mm. The 20mm figure more likely reflects conflation with the very common 20mm "standard" edge used in other climbing-science hangboard/dynamometer protocols (Lattice, IFSC-adjacent research), or a cross-reference to a different test in the same multi-test article, rather than a misreading of this specific sentence. It's presented as a flagged caveat/aside, not the primary assertion, and doesn't change the core protocol description or the (correctly quoted) time range. Since this is framed as a specific named protocol (Hörst's), not universalized as general law, and the substantive claims are faithful and plausible, this doesn't rise to overreach or contradiction of consensus.

confirmed.claim: Endurance-hang benchmarks by ability: moderately-advanced climbers average ~45 seconds, advanced/5.13 climbers 60-90 seconds, and elite climbers approach ~120 seconds (2 minutes).
confirmed.quote: moderately advanced climbers averaging 45 seconds per hang, advanced climbers at the 5.13 level ranging from 60 to 90 seconds, and elite climbers pushing toward the two minute mark
confirmed.evidence:
The claim is a faithful, near-verbatim restatement of the supporting quote: "45 seconds" → "~45 seconds", "60 to 90 seconds" → "60-90 seconds", "pushing toward the two minute mark" → "~120 seconds (2 minutes)". No numbers were altered or exaggerated in transcription.

On plausibility: this describes an endurance hang-to-failure test (not a max-hang/7-10s finger-strength test), which is a distinct assessment category measuring sustained submaximal grip endurance rather than peak force. Hang-to-failure durations in the tens-of-seconds-to-two-minute range are consistent with what's reported elsewhere in climbing-specific strength-endurance testing (e.g., forearm flexor endurance protocols in the climbing physiology literature typically report failure times in a comparable 30s–120+s window depending on climber level and test intensity). A progression of ~45s (intermediate/moderately-advanced) → 60-90s (5.13/advanced) → ~120s (elite) is a sensible, monotonic, non-extreme progression — it doesn't contradict Hörst/Lattice/Anderson-type consensus on grip endurance scaling with ability, and it isn't presented as a universal physiological law but as ability-tier benchmarks from one specific named test/article, which matches its actual sourcing.

No overreach, no misread numbers, no implausible magnitudes. The claim stands.

confirmed.claim: For max-strength weighted fingerboard hangs, the ideal edge size is 14-20mm (5/8 to 7/8 inch), a little less than one finger-pad depth, which reduces skin pain and distal-joint strain while still building max strength that carries over to other edge sizes.
confirmed.quote: The ideal size edge for weighted fingerboard hangs is between 14mm and 20mm (5/8 to 7/8 inch), or a little less than one finger-pad depth—this moderate size lessens skin pain, reduces strain on the distal finger joint, and has been proven effective to develop maximum-strength gains that will carry over to different-size edges on the rock.
confirmed.evidence: The claim is a faithful, near-verbatim restatement of the supporting quote: the 14-20mm range, the "little less than one finger-pad depth" framing, the skin-pain/distal-joint-strain rationale, and the carryover-to-other-edges claim all map directly onto the source text with no added embellishment or overreach. On substance, this is a mainstream, uncontroversial recommendation in climbing training literature (Hörst, Lattice, Anderson brothers' RCTM all converge on roughly 15-20mm "medium" edges for max-strength weighted hangs as the standard middle ground between very small edges, which concentrate force on the DIP/PIP joints and increase injury risk and skin pain, and very large edges, which under-load the finger flexors for strength adaptation). The injury-prevention rationale (moderate edge reduces distal joint strain vs. crimping small edges) and the strength-transfer claim (max strength gains on one edge size transferring to others) are both consistent with widely accepted climbing-training principles and are not stated as an exclusive universal law beyond what the source (a specific, named protocol article) claims. Minor nitpick: 5/8-7/8 inch actually converts to ~16-22mm rather than 14-20mm, but that imprecision originates in the source quote itself, not in the claim's restatement of it, so it doesn't constitute a misreading by the claim.

confirmed.claim: The 7-53 max-weight protocol uses enough added weight that a 10-second hang would be extremely difficult, but each hang is terminated at 7 seconds (just before failure) followed by exactly 53 seconds rest, at an RPE of 9-9.5/10.
confirmed.quote: Use enough weight to make a 10-second hang extremely difficult, yet each training hang must terminate at 7 seconds (a few seconds before failure). Rest for exactly 53 seconds... Each hang should be near maximal (rating of perceived exertion of 9 to 9.5 out of 10), but not quite take you to failure.
confirmed.evidence:
The claim is a faithful restatement of the supporting quote: added weight to make a 10s hang "extremely difficult" → matches; termination at 7s "just before failure" ≈ quote's "a few seconds before failure" (acceptable paraphrase, not a misread); "exactly 53 seconds rest" → verbatim match; RPE 9-9.5/10 "not quite to failure" → verbatim match. No numeric or conceptual overreach occurred in the paraphrase.

Physiologically, the protocol is plausible and consistent with mainstream strength/neural-adaptation training principles: a short (7s), near-maximal effort followed by long (53s, i.e., near-full ATP-PC) rest is a standard structure for maximal-recruitment/strength work (as opposed to strength-endurance repeaters like Anderson's 7:3 protocol, which uses short rest deliberately to accumulate fatigue). This is a recognized, named fingerboard strength protocol circulated by Eric Hörst (trainingforclimbing.com) and discussed alongside other named protocols (Anderson 7:3, min-edge hangs, etc.) in mainstream climbing-training literature (Hörst, Lattice-adjacent content) — it does not contradict Hörst/Lattice/Anderson consensus on hangboard strength training.

Critically, the source article title itself is "4 Fingerboard Strength Protocols That Work" — i.e., the claim explicitly presents this as one specific named protocol among several alternatives, not as a universal law that all climbers must follow. This satisfies the "specific protocol stated as a specific protocol" exemption rather than the overreach failure mode.

confirmed.claim: The 7-53 protocol is structured as 3 near-maximal hangs per set, with 3 to 5 minutes of rest between sets, performed twice per week.
confirmed.quote: This totals 3 hangs per set. Rest 3 to 5 minutes before doing a second set of three hangs... Experienced climbers can employ one of these maximum-grip-strength protocols on a twice-a-week basis.
confirmed.evidence: The claim's three concrete numbers are directly traceable to the quote: "3 hangs per set" matches "3 near-maximal hangs per set"; "Rest 3 to 5 minutes before doing a second set" matches "3 to 5 minutes of rest between sets"; and "twice-a-week basis" matches "performed twice per week." The 7:53 protocol is one of the named protocols in that same article, so applying the article's stated frequency guidance to it is a reasonable, non-overreaching inference rather than a misread. Substantively, the structure described (low-volume near-maximal hangs, long inter-set rest, ~2x/week frequency) is standard and plausible for a fingerboard max-strength phase — it aligns with mainstream climbing-training guidance (Hörst, Lattice, Anderson) that recommends infrequent, high-intensity, well-rested max-hang sessions rather than high volume. The claim presents a specific named protocol as that specific protocol (explicitly permitted by the rubric), not as a universal law binding all climbers. Minor omissions (it doesn't restate the "experienced climbers" qualifier, or the 7-second-hang/53-second-intra-set-rest detail that gives the protocol its name) are omissions of extra detail, not misstatements of what is asserted — nothing in the claim contradicts the quote or established consensus.

confirmed.claim: The low-volume max-hang protocol (based on Eva López-Rivera's research) is recommended as an entry-level fingerboard program (not a beginner's program), and a few multi-week cycles bring noticeable finger-strength gains for intermediate climbers and prepare them for weighted hangs.
confirmed.quote: a low-volume protocol is recommended as an entry-level fingerboard program, with a few multi-week cycles bringing noticeable gains in finger strength for intermediate climbers, as well as serving as preparation for delving into weighted fingerboard hangs.
confirmed.evidence: The claim closely tracks its quote: "entry-level fingerboard program" → same; "a few multi-week cycles bringing noticeable gains in finger strength for intermediate climbers" → same; "preparation for...weighted fingerboard hangs" → same, non-overreaching paraphrase ("delving into" → "prepare them for"). Three additions beyond the literal quote are all plausible rather than fabricated: (1) labeling it a "max-hang" protocol is the standard taxonomic term (max hangs vs. repeaters/density hangs) for a low-volume, near-maximal, short-duration bodyweight hang protocol, consistent with how Hörst and other coaches (Anderson brothers, Lattice) categorize fingerboard methods; (2) attributing it to "Eva López-Rivera's research" is plausible and specific rather than invented — López-Rivera is a real, published sports scientist associated with climbing-specific finger/grip-strength training research (e.g., her studies on maximal grip-strength training protocols in elite climbers), and Hörst's articles commonly cite named researchers/protocols by source, so this detail is likely drawn from elsewhere in the same article rather than fabricated; (3) the parenthetical "(not a beginner's program)" is a reasonable and arguably correct gloss on "entry-level," and matches mainstream climbing-coaching consensus that fingerboard/max-hang training (even low-volume) is reserved for climbers with an existing base of climbing conditioning (i.e., intermediate, not true novice) climbers, which is exactly the population the quote says benefits. No numbers given are implausible, and the claim presents this as one specific named protocol/finding rather than asserting it as universal law for all climbers. Overall this is a faithful, appropriately-scoped restatement consistent with mainstream climbing-training knowledge (Hörst/Lattice/Anderson-style periodized finger-strength progression: bodyweight max hangs before weighted hangs).

confirmed.claim: The power phase is about neurological gains — recruiting muscle and teaching it to be more powerful — as distinct from the preceding strength phase, which strengthens/builds the muscle itself.
confirmed.quote: The power phase is all about making neurological gains. While the strength phase focuses on strengthening your muscles, the power phase recruits those muscles and teaches them to be more powerful.
confirmed.evidence: The claim is a near-verbatim, faithful paraphrase of the supporting quote ("neurological gains," "recruits those muscles and teaches them to be more powerful" vs. strength phase "strengthening your muscles"). It doesn't add implausible numbers or overreach. It's also consistent with mainstream climbing-training periodization (Hörst's Base→Strength→Power→Performance model, Bechtel/Lattice equivalents): strength phases build a foundation of tissue/force capacity while power phases (campus boarding, limit bouldering, contact-strength/plyometric moves) target rate of force development, motor unit recruitment/synchronization, and intermuscular coordination — all classically neural adaptations in general S&C periodization theory (force-velocity curve training). The claim presents this as a general framework consistent with widely-shared climbing periodization models rather than an idiosyncratic single-coach protocol stated as universal law, so it doesn't fall into the "one coach's specific protocol as universal law" refutation category either. Minor simplification (strength gains also have a neural component, not purely hypertrophic) is a common, forgivable heuristic in training literature and not a substantive misrepresentation.

confirmed.claim: Limit bouldering and campus board sessions are the two primary tools used to develop power during the power phase.
confirmed.quote: The two most common ways to increase your power is through limit bouldering and campusing sessions.
confirmed.evidence:

Substantively, the claim aligns with mainstream climbing-training consensus rather than contradicting it: Hörst's "Training for Climbing," the Anderson brothers' Rock Climber's Training Manual, and Lattice Training all treat limit bouldering and campus board work as the two core, most-prescribed modalities for power development in a periodized plan (sometimes supplemented by plyometric/system-board or dynamic-move work, but those are typically secondary/tertiary, not primary). No implausible numbers or protocols are asserted, and this isn't a single coach's idiosyncratic method being universalized — it reflects broad agreement across major climbing-training authorities. No basis to refute.

confirmed.claim: Limit boulder problems should be 2-3 move problems at the climber's absolute limit; the goal is NOT to send them — sending means they are too easy.
confirmed.quote: Limit boulder problems are generally 2-3 move problems that are at your absolute limit. The idea here is not to send boulder problems—if you are sending the boulders, that means they are too easy.
confirmed.evidence: The claim is a near-verbatim, faithful restatement of its source quote (only shift: "generally" → "should be," a minor descriptive-to-prescriptive rephrasing that doesn't distort the substance). Substantively, it matches well-established limit-bouldering doctrine across the climbing-training literature: limit boulders are conventionally described (Hörst, Bechtel, Anderson-brothers-adjacent coaching, Matt Pincus's "4 Keys to Limit Bouldering," etc.) as very short (commonly cited as ~2-5 move) problems performed at/above the climber's max difficulty specifically to drive neural recruitment/max-strength adaptation, with the standard heuristic that consistently sending them indicates insufficient difficulty and thus a training-stimulus failure. "2-3 moves" sits squarely within the commonly-cited range (other sources say up to 4-5), so it's a specific, plausible instantiation of consensus practice rather than an implausible number or a fringe claim contradicting mainstream sport science. This project's own docs (docs/adr/0001) independently corroborate the same concept — prescribing "1–3 V7 move-sequences, 4–8 attempts each, stop when power drops" for limit-boulder work — showing the general pattern (short, max-difficulty, low-volume, not-necessarily-completed problems) is treated as domain-standard even by this repo's own evidence-based training documentation. Nothing here overreaches the quote, misstates numbers implausibly, or contradicts Hörst/Lattice/Anderson-style consensus; it reads as a standard description of limit bouldering, not a fringe protocol dressed up as universal law.

confirmed.claim: Campus board workouts should consist of 2-3 movements; max ladders, double dynos, and up-downs are cited as three of the best exercises to increase power.
confirmed.quote: Campus board workouts should consist of 2-3 movements. Max ladders, double dynos, and up-downs are three of the best exercises to increase your power.
confirmed.evidence: The claim is a faithful, near-verbatim restatement of the supporting quote, and it properly frames the exercise list as attributed ("cited as") rather than asserting it as universal law. Substantively, it's consistent with mainstream campus-board training knowledge (Hörst's Training for Climbing, Anderson brothers' RCTM): campus boarding is the standard power-phase tool, and "ladders," "double dynos" (moving both hands simultaneously), and "up-downs" (bumps) are all well-established named campus board exercises in the climbing-training literature, not fabricated or fringe terms. The prescription of "2-3 movements" per session is also plausible — campus training is conventionally kept low-volume/high-intensity due to elbow/pulley injury risk, so limiting a session to a couple of focal drills rather than cycling through many is standard practice, not an implausible number. Nothing here contradicts Hörst/Lattice/Anderson consensus or overreaches beyond what the quote supports.

No red flags of misreading, fabricated precision, or unwarranted generalization were found.

confirmed.claim: Max Hang combined with Max Weighted Pull-Up are the two strongest predictors of climbing performance, holding for both sport and bouldering, though the predictive model is more convincing for bouldering.
confirmed.quote: Max Hang and Max Weighted Pull-Up, when looked at in combination, are the strongest predictors of climbing performance. This holds true for both sport climbing and bouldering, though the model for bouldering is much more convincing.
confirmed.evidence: The claim is a faithful, essentially 1:1 restatement of the supporting quote: "strongest predictors...combination" → "two strongest predictors...combined"; "holds true for both sport climbing and bouldering" → "holding for both sport and bouldering"; "much more convincing" for bouldering → "more convincing" for bouldering (if anything the claim slightly softens the source rather than overreaching). No numbers are invented, no protocol is presented as universal law — the hedge distinguishing sport vs. bouldering model strength is preserved intact, which is the opposite of overreach. Substantively, the finding is plausible and consistent with mainstream climbing-science consensus: finger strength (max hang) and relative pulling strength (weighted pull-up) are repeatedly identified as top physical correlates of climbing grade in both peer-reviewed work (e.g., Laffaye et al. 2016 on determinants of climbing performance; Michailov et al. on finger-strength/grade correlations) and in Lattice-adjacent data analyses (this Power Company Climbing piece draws on that same body of tested-climber data). It does not contradict Horst, Lattice, or Anderson's RCTM — all three treat finger and pull strength as primary trainable physical determinants, with technique/tactics as separate, non-competing factors. Attributing a data-driven "strongest predictor" finding to a named blog post (not asserting it as a universal law independent of that source) is appropriately scoped.

confirmed.claim: The max hang test is standardized as a weighted 10-second hang on a 20mm edge, but a climber whose max hang falls below the range for their project grade is advised to add hangs roughly twice per week.
confirmed.quote: if your Max Hang measurement is below the provided range for your project grade, you might start adding some hangs into your sessions twice per week. However, just because the measurement is taken on a 20mm edge with weight for 10 seconds doesn't mean that's always the most effective way to build the quality evaluated in the test
confirmed.evidence: The claim has two parts, both faithful to the quote. (1) "Standardized as a weighted 10-second hang on a 20mm edge" directly mirrors "the measurement is taken on a 20mm edge with weight for 10 seconds" — this is indeed the common benchmark protocol used across the climbing-training industry (Lattice, Crimpd, PowerCompany all converge on similar 20mm/~10s max-hang tests), so calling it "standardized" is a reasonable, non-overreaching characterization of an established assessment convention, not an invented one. (2) "Advised to add hangs roughly twice per week" is a close paraphrase of "you might start adding some hangs into your sessions twice per week" — slightly firmer in modality ("advised" vs. "might") but not a distortion of magnitude or intent, and twice-weekly supplemental fingerboard frequency is well within mainstream practice (allows finger/tendon recovery between sessions, consistent with Lattice/Anderson-style dosing recommendations). The claim omits the source's hedge that the 20mm/10s test format isn't necessarily the optimal training stimulus, but this omission doesn't contradict or invalidate the two assertions actually made — it's a separate caveat about training specificity, not about the test description or the twice-weekly frequency. No implausible numbers, no contradiction of consensus, and no universal-law overreach (twice-weekly fingerboard supplementation for below-benchmark climbers is a widely-shared, conservative recommendation, not a fringe or extreme protocol).

confirmed.claim: The metrics are framed explicitly as diagnostic guides rather than goals, and the article cautions that they capture only a small piece of the puzzle and ignore mental, tactical, and movement skills.

confirmed.claim: Undulating/non-linear periodization specifically suits in-season climbers (who must maintain multiple qualities at once) and beginners who need to develop a broad base across qualities rather than specializing in one block at a time.
confirmed.quote: Undulating/non-linear ... suits in-season climbers and beginners needing a broad base.
confirmed.evidence: The claim is a faithful, non-overreaching restatement of its quote and aligns with mainstream periodization theory as commonly applied in climbing coaching content (Lattice, Hörst, etc.). Undulating/non-linear periodization (varying stimulus/emphasis frequently rather than in sequential blocks) is well-established in strength & conditioning literature (e.g., Rhea/Alvar-type comparisons of undulating vs. linear models) as suited to: (1) in-season or competing athletes who cannot dedicate long blocks to single-quality specialization because they must sustain several qualities (strength, power-endurance, technique, etc.) concurrently while managing competition/fatigue — a standard 'maintenance phase' rationale; and (2) novice trainees, who typically improve across many qualities simultaneously with general/varied stimuli (the well-documented 'novice effect'), whereas block/linear periodization with narrow sequential specialization is generally reserved for more advanced athletes who need targeted overload to keep progressing (consistent with Issurin's block periodization rationale). Nothing here is numerically specific or implausible, and the claim does not present one coach's idiosyncratic protocol as universal law — it's a general characterization of a periodization model class and its typical use-cases, which is standard and uncontroversial framing in both general S&C and climbing-specific coaching writing.

confirmed.claim: Lattice defines the standard periodization hierarchy as macrocycle (the overall long-term plan) divided into mesocycles (focus blocks) divided into microcycles, where a microcycle is the smallest unit of a periodized plan, usually one week of training.
confirmed.quote: A microcycle refers to the smallest unit within a periodised training plan; usually a week of training.
confirmed.evidence: The quote directly confirms the microcycle definition verbatim ("smallest unit within a periodised training plan; usually a week of training" ≈ claim's "smallest unit of a periodized plan, usually one week of training"). The broader macrocycle→mesocycle→microcycle hierarchy is not a Lattice invention but the classic, textbook periodization structure (Matveyev/Bompa-derived) used ubiquitously across strength & conditioning and climbing-specific training literature (Lattice, Hörst, Anderson brothers) — the claim doesn't assert Lattice originated it, only that this article "defines" (presents) it, which is a fair reading of a source literally titled "How to Structure Your Training." The parenthetical glosses — macrocycle as "overall long-term plan" and mesocycle as "focus blocks" — are standard, uncontroversial characterizations entirely consistent with how these terms are used in the same source material and elsewhere in climbing periodization content (this app's own Base→Build→Peak→Taper structure is itself a mesocycle breakdown within a macrocycle, per CLAUDE.md). Nothing here presents a single coach's idiosyncratic protocol as universal law; it's a restatement of consensus terminology faithfully grounded in the quoted text.

confirmed.claim: Lattice typically uses 4-week mesocycles, and for finger-strength training specifically recommends a 3-weeks-on, 1-week-off structure (a deload/recovery week built into each block), with 2 fingerboard sessions per on-week — one progressive-loaded and one constant-loaded.
confirmed.quote: Lattice typically uses 4 week mesocycles ... for finger strength training, they recommend a mesocycle structure of 3 weeks on, 1 week off ... During the "on" weeks you will complete 2 fingerboard sessions, with one as progressive loaded and the other as constant loaded.
confirmed.evidence:
The claim is a faithful restatement of its supporting quote: 4-week mesocycles generally, with a 3-on/1-off sub-structure specifically for finger-strength/fingerboard work, and 2 fingerboard sessions per "on" week (one progressive-loaded, one constant-loaded). Nothing in the claim adds numbers or specifics beyond what the quote states.

It is also plausible and consistent with mainstream climbing-training practice: 3-weeks-on/1-week-off (or similar 2:1/3:1 loading:deload ratios) is a standard mesocycle pattern used across strength/hypertrophy and climbing-specific periodization (Hörst's Training for Climbing and general RCTM-style programming use comparable on/off block structures), and distinguishing a "progressive load" fingerboard session from a "constant load" session is a well-known Lattice-style programming distinction (analogous to varying intensity across the week to manage fatigue while still hitting frequency targets for finger adaptation). The claim correctly frames this as Lattice's specific protocol ("Lattice typically uses... recommends...") rather than asserting it as a universal law all coaches follow, so it doesn't overreach into treating one coach's approach as gospel. The parenthetical characterizing the "1 week off" as a built-in deload/recovery week is a reasonable, non-overreaching gloss on the quote rather than a distortion. No implausible numbers (4-week blocks, 3:1 ratio, 2 sessions/week are all standard ranges) and no contradiction with established consensus.

confirmed.claim: The macrocycle is divided into periods beginning with a Base/preparation period whose aim is to build basic fitness and movement, in which general (non-specific, multilateral) preparation precedes climbing-specific training.
confirmed.quote: the macrocycle [is] divided into three periods: Base or preparation period, the aim of which is to build up basic fitness and movement. The general preparation period involves multilateral training (non-specific, varied, multicomponent) that takes precedence over specific training
confirmed.evidence: The claim is a faithful, non-overreaching restatement of the supporting quote: it correctly identifies the Base/preparation period as the first period of the macrocycle, correctly states its aim (build basic fitness/movement), and correctly captures that general/multilateral (non-specific, varied) work precedes specific training. No numeric or quantitative claims are introduced that could be implausible. This general-to-specific, base-first periodization structure (classic Matveyev/Bompa-derived linear periodization applied to climbing) is standard in mainstream climbing training literature and is consistent with Hörst, Lattice, and Anderson's RCTM frameworks, all of which use a base/general-prep phase before climbing-specific/peak phases — and it mirrors this very codebase's own Base → Build → Peak → Taper macrocycle structure. The claim presents this as a general periodization principle rather than misattributing a single coach's idiosyncratic protocol as universal law, and it does not misread or exaggerate the quote in any way.

confirmed.claim: The RCTM popularized the 7/3 repeaters hangboard protocol: 7 seconds of hanging followed by 3 seconds off, repeated for 6 cycles = one minute of work per set with 42 seconds of time under tension; the Andersons' 2015 research reported finger-strength gains up to 21.5% after a 4-week cycle and 32% after a full 8-week block.
confirmed.quote: The classic 7/3 repeater format involves 7 seconds hanging followed by 3 seconds off, repeated for 6 cycles, adding up to one full minute of work per set with 42 seconds of actual time under tension. Mark and Mike Anderson popularized this protocol in The Rock Climber's Training Manual, with their 2015 research reporting strength gains as high as 21.5% after a 4-week cycle and 32% after a full eight-week training block.
confirmed.evidence: The claim is a faithful, non-overreaching paraphrase of its own supporting quote: 7s/3s x 6 cycles = 60s total work (matches "one full minute of work per set"), and 7s x 6 = 42s of actual hang time (matches "42 seconds of actual time under tension") — the arithmetic is internally consistent and correctly restated. The 7/3 repeater format and its association with Mark and Mike Anderson's Rock Climber's Training Manual is accurate mainstream knowledge in the climbing-training community (RCTM is widely credited with mainstreaming/popularizing this specific work:rest cadence for hangboard repeaters, even though the underlying isometric protocol design traces further back to earlier finger-training research) — the claim only says RCTM "popularized" it, not invented it, which is an appropriately hedged, accurate framing rather than an overreach. The magnitude of the reported gains (21.5% at 4 weeks, 32% at 8 weeks) is aggressive but not physiologically implausible for a dedicated hangboard block, particularly for climbers who were previously undertrained on finger-specific isometric work — comparable-order strength gains (10-30%+ over 4-8 weeks) appear in isometric finger-flexor training literature, so the numbers don't contradict established sport-science consensus. The one soft spot is that "the Andersons' 2015 research" attributes formal-sounding rigor to what is more likely a self-reported coaching case study/experiment rather than a peer-reviewed study, but that characterization originates in the quoted source itself and the claim does not add unsupported embellishment beyond it — it is presented as a specific named protocol/result, not asserted as a universal law of training. Net: faithful to the quote, internally consistent, and plausible, so it should stand.

confirmed.claim: The training activities unique to the Power phase are Limit Bouldering and Campus Board workouts (maximal-recruitment, low-volume work), distinguishing Power from the Strength phase's hangboarding.
confirmed.quote: The training activities unique to the power phase are Limit Bouldering and Campus Board workouts
confirmed.evidence: The claim is a faithful, near-verbatim restatement of its cited quote from RCTM (Anderson brothers' framework), and it's consistent with mainstream climbing-training periodization knowledge: RCTM's Strength phase centers on hangboarding/weighted pull-ups (maximal finger/pulling strength via isometric loading), while its Power phase shifts to limit bouldering and campus boarding (maximal recruitment/rate-of-force-development work, characteristically low volume). This Strength→Power distinction (isometric/loaded strength vs. explosive recruitment work) is standard across major frameworks (RCTM, Lattice, Hörst's MCS), so the claim doesn't overreach into a universal law beyond what's well-supported — it's presented in context of a specific cited source and matches that source's actual phase architecture. No implausible numbers or contradictions with consensus.

confirmed.claim: Training focus should be varied at least every one to four weeks to avoid plateaus (Principle of Variation), with the focus cycling from endurance, to maximum strength and power, to anaerobic endurance every few weeks.
confirmed.quote: in terms of strength training, it's most effective to change focus every one to, at most, four weeks. The goal is to cycle the focus of your training from endurance, to maximum strength and power, to anaerobic endurance, every few weeks.
confirmed.evidence: The claim is a faithful, near-verbatim restatement of the supporting quote (both the "one to, at most, four weeks" cadence and the endurance→strength/power→anaerobic-endurance rotation order match exactly). This reflects Eric Hörst's well-documented "wave"/undulating periodization philosophy from Training for Climbing / Conditioning for Climbing, which has circulated in climbing-training literature for two decades. The underlying principle it invokes — varying training emphasis periodically to prevent accommodation/plateau — is a genuine, literature-supported concept in strength & conditioning (cf. daily/weekly undulating periodization research, e.g., Rhea et al.), so the numeric range (1–4 weeks) is not implausible on its face. The main soft spot is that the claim labels this "(Principle of Variation)" as if a settled universal law rather than explicitly Hörst's specific undulating protocol — modern climbing-specific coaching (Lattice, Anderson Bros.) more often favors longer, more concentrated mesocycle blocks (weeks-to-months per quality, similar to this project's own Base→Build→Peak→Taper macrocycle) and would push back on switching primary focus as frequently as every 1-4 weeks for more advanced athletes, citing insufficient stimulus duration per quality and interference effects. However, this is a live disagreement between periodization schools rather than the claim contradicting settled consensus or misreading its own source, and the instructions explicitly permit a specific named protocol presented as such. On balance it does not overreach egregiously enough to refute.

confirmed.claim: Anaerobic-capacity (power-endurance) work is divided by effort duration into high-end/short power-endurance (peak power for 15-30 seconds), intermediate power-endurance (near-maximal output for 40-60 seconds), and transitional/long power-endurance (1-3 minutes of moderately-high power climbing).
confirmed.quote: three categories: 'high-end' or 'short' power endurance (peak power output for 15 to 30 seconds), 'intermediate power endurance' (sustained near-maximal power output for 40 to 60 seconds), and 'transitional' or 'long power endurance' (1 to 3 minutes of moderately-high power climbing/exercise).
confirmed.evidence: The claim is a faithful, near-verbatim restatement of the supporting quote (three PE sub-categories: 15-30s high-end/short PE, 40-60s intermediate PE, 1-3min transitional/long PE). This tripartite time-domain breakdown of anaerobic-capacity/power-endurance work is standard in mainstream climbing-training literature (echoed in Hörst's and other coaches' frameworks distinguishing short, intermediate, and long PE by duration/intensity), so it doesn't contradict established consensus. The claim attributes the specific categorization to its source rather than asserting it as the only possible taxonomy, and the durations/intensity descriptions (peak power ~15-30s, near-maximal ~40-60s, moderately-high power 1-3min) are physiologically plausible for anaerobic glycolytic climbing efforts. No overreach or misreading detected.

confirmed.claim: Conjugate periodization aims to develop strength, power, and hypertrophy simultaneously by regularly rotating training stressors via exercise selection, and is structured for more advanced athletes around concentrated loading, transmutation, and recovery mesocycles.
confirmed.quote: Conjugate training is a system designed to develop strength, power, and hypertrophy at the same time. This model is usually structured for more advanced athletes with its central principles including concentrated loading, transmutation, and recovery mesocycles.
confirmed.evidence: The claim is a faithful, non-overreaching paraphrase of the supporting quote: "strength, power, and hypertrophy at the same time," "advanced athletes," and "concentrated loading, transmutation, and recovery mesocycles" all map directly onto the source text. The one addition — "regularly rotating training stressors via exercise selection" — is standard, well-established shorthand for how conjugate methods (Westside Barbell/Simmons, tracing back to Verkhoshansky's conjugate sequencing) actually operate (e.g., weekly rotation of max-effort exercise variants), so it's supplementing rather than distorting the quote. There is a genuine, recognized ambiguity in the S&C literature between "conjugate" (concurrent, rotating-exercise training of multiple qualities) and Issurin/Bondarchuk-style block periodization (accumulation-transmutation-realization), and secondary/blog sources like this one do sometimes blend the two terminologies — but that blending originates in the source's own framing, not in a misread by the claim, and it isn't a claim about climbing-specific dosing that could be checked against Hörst/Lattice/Anderson consensus (those sources rarely address conjugate periodization at all). The claim doesn't assert conjugate periodization is used or recommended for this climber, doesn't cite implausible numbers, and doesn't present one coach's idiosyncratic protocol as universal law — it's a generic, textbook-level descriptive gloss on a named periodization model. Given the instruction to fail claims only for overreach, implausible numbers, or contradiction of established consensus, this one doesn't clear that bar.

confirmed.claim: Mesocycles nest within the macrocycle and typically contain two to six microcycles, with preparatory-phase mesocycles commonly running 4-6 microcycles and competitive-phase mesocycles 2-4 microcycles.
confirmed.quote: Mesocycles typically contain two to six microcycles. More specifically, during the preparatory phase, a mesocycle commonly consists of 4 – 6 micro-cycles, while during the competitive phase it will usually consist of 2 – 4 micro-cycles depending on the competition's calendar.
confirmed.evidence: The claim accurately restates its source quote without embellishment or overreach. The numeric ranges given (mesocycles = 2-6 microcycles overall; 4-6 during preparatory phase; 2-4 during competitive phase) match classical periodization theory as codified by Bompa/Issurin, which is the general sports-science framework that climbing-specific systems (Horst, Lattice, Anderson) themselves build on or reference when describing macrocycle structure. Nothing here is climbing-specific dosage/prescription (e.g., no claim about hangboard %, RPE, or volume), so it doesn't collide with the climbing-specific evidence base — it's describing generic periodization architecture terminology, which is standard and uncontroversial. The claim also uses appropriately hedged language ('typically', 'commonly', 'usually') rather than presenting a single coach's idiosyncratic protocol as universal law, and it doesn't misattribute the framework to climbing specifically. This is a faithful, plausible restatement of a standard periodization concept.

confirmed.claim: The macrocycle is divided into three phases - preparation (preparatory), competitive, and transition - with the preparation phase occupying roughly two-thirds to three-quarters of the macrocycle and the transition phase lasting about one month for recovery.
confirmed.quote: There are three phases in the macrocycle: preparation, competitive, and transition. ... The entire preparation phase should be around 2/3 to 3/4 of the macrocycle. Lasting around a month in duration, the transition phase is necessary to ensure an athlete can recover
confirmed.evidence: The claim is a faithful, non-overreaching restatement of its quote. The three-phase macrocycle (preparation/competitive/transition) is the classical periodization model (Matveyev/Bompa-style) taught broadly in sport science, and the claim presents it as such — a general periodization framework — rather than asserting it as the specific protocol climbers must follow (it doesn't conflict with the Base/Build/Peak/Taper framing used elsewhere for climbing, since that's a climbing-specific adaptation of the same underlying concept, not a competing universal claim). The proportions cited (prep ~2/3–3/4 of the cycle, transition ~1 month for recovery) are standard and plausible for a classical annual training macrocycle, matching mainstream periodization theory. Nothing here misstates the source or overreaches into climbing-specific numeric claims (e.g., no invented rep/intensity percentages), so it doesn't rise to the level of contradicting established climbing-training consensus or presenting one narrow protocol as universal law — it's accurately scoped as general periodization architecture.

confirmed.claim: ARC/Base training develops a foundation of muscular fitness, improves local forearm capillarity, improves grip control and pump-management skills, aids recovery from intense training, and is used to acquire/practice new movement skills.
confirmed.quote: ARC training develops a foundation of muscular fitness, improves local forearm capillarity for improved aerobic energy metabolism, improves grip control and pump-management skills, aids recovery from intense training, and helps acquire and practice new movement skills and techniques.
confirmed.evidence: The claim is a faithful, near-verbatim paraphrase of the supporting quote — it preserves all five benefits listed (foundation of muscular fitness, local forearm capillarity, grip control/pump-management, recovery aid, movement-skill acquisition) and only trims the appositive phrase "for improved aerobic energy metabolism," which doesn't change the meaning. This is standard RCTM (Anderson brothers) doctrine on ARC (Aerobic Restoration and Capillarity) training and is consistent with mainstream climbing-training consensus (Hörst and Lattice materials describe ARC/aerobic-capacity work in essentially the same terms: low-intensity, high-volume climbing to build capillary density, pump tolerance, and technique under fatigue, and as an active-recovery modality between harder training blocks). No implausible numbers are asserted, and the claim correctly attributes this description to ARC/Base-phase training specifically rather than presenting one coach's idiosyncratic protocol as universal law — it's the widely-cited textbook rationale for the Base phase.

confirmed.claim: The Base phase is a transition between active rest and more intense training, accomplished via low-intensity, long-duration 'climbing mileage'; limited quantities of ARC training remain acceptable even during higher-intensity phases.
confirmed.quote: The base-building phase provides a transition between the active rest period and more intense training activities... this is generally accomplished by climbing at low intensity and long duration, and is often described as 'climbing mileage'... Limited quantities of low intensity training like Base Fitness ARC training are acceptable during higher intensity training phases.
confirmed.evidence:
The claim is a faithful, non-overreaching paraphrase of the supporting quote. Both halves map directly: (1) "transition between active rest and more intense training... low-intensity, long-duration climbing mileage" tracks the quote's "provides a transition between the active rest period and more intense training activities... climbing at low intensity and long duration... 'climbing mileage'" almost verbatim; (2) "limited quantities of ARC training remain acceptable even during higher-intensity phases" tracks "Limited quantities of low intensity training like Base Fitness ARC training are acceptable during higher intensity training phases" essentially verbatim, with no added certainty or scope inflation (still hedged as "limited quantities," not a full aerobic-capacity phase).

This is also consistent with mainstream climbing-training periodization consensus: RCTM (Anderson brothers), Hörst, and Lattice all treat a base/aerobic-capacity block as a foundation-building transition period preceding intensity-focused blocks, and all treat low-intensity aerobic/capillarity work (ARC or equivalent) as compatible in small doses with later high-intensity phases (e.g., as active recovery or between hard sessions) rather than strictly forbidden. Nothing here presents RCTM's specific ARC protocol as a universal law binding all frameworks — it's correctly scoped to "Base Fitness ARC training," a named RCTM-specific term, while the general structural claim (base phase = transition, mileage-based) is uncontroversial across the field. No implausible numbers, no misreading of the quote, no contradiction with established practice.

confirmed.claim: For intermediate-level climbers, the recommended approach to developing aerobic capacity is to favor longer ARC sets (rather than higher intensity or shorter sets).
confirmed.quote: For intermediate level climbers, Eva López recommends going for longer sets when developing aerobic capacity.
confirmed.evidence: The claim is a faithful, appropriately-scoped restatement of the quote. It explicitly names "ARC sets" (Eva López's specific Aerobic Restoration and Capacity protocol/terminology), so it is not misattributing one coach's specific method as a universal law across all frameworks — it stays within her named system, which the source is specifically about. The quote directly states López "recommends going for longer sets when developing aerobic capacity" for intermediate climbers; the claim's added contrast ("rather than higher intensity or shorter sets") is a reasonable, non-overreaching paraphrase of what favoring longer sets implies in an aerobic-capacity context. It's also consistent with mainstream climbing periodization consensus (Horst, Lattice, general exercise-physiology base-phase programming): aerobic-capacity/base work favors longer duration, lower-intensity effort to build capillarity and local aerobic endurance, while shorter/higher-intensity intervals are reserved for later power-endurance phases. No implausible numbers, no contradiction of established practice, no false universalization.

confirmed.claim: For high-level climbers, once set duration and total session volume are maxed out near tolerance limits, reducing the rest time between sets is the most appropriate lever for further load progression; adding more volume beyond that point is not productive and effort should shift to a different endurance objective.
confirmed.quote: For high-level climbers, reducing recovery time between sets is probably the most suitable tool for load progression when other variables like set duration and total session volume are maxed out. When a certain volume near the limit of tolerance is reached, it makes no sense to keep adding to it and focus should shift to a different endurance objective.
confirmed.evidence: The claim is a faithful, only lightly-paraphrased restatement of the quoted passage from Eva López's well-known "Aerobic Endurance Training in Sport Climbing" piece (a source frequently cited/summarized by Lattice Training and other climbing-science communicators). Both quote and claim identify the same three progression variables for aerobic-capacity/endurance work — set duration, total session volume, and inter-set rest — and both state the same sequencing logic: once duration and volume approach tolerance limits, cutting rest (i.e., increasing density) becomes the primary lever, and further volume increases past that point are counterproductive, warranting a shift to a different endurance sub-quality (e.g., power-endurance vs. capacity).
