The claim drops the hedge word "probably" but keeps "most appropriate/suitable," which is not a meaningful overreach — it doesn't convert a specific protocol into a rigid universal law, nor does it introduce implausible numbers or contradict mainstream frameworks (Hörst's ARC progression, Lattice's density-based endurance programming, and general strength/endurance periodization all use the same density-progression logic once volume plateaus). Nothing here conflicts with established climbing-training consensus for base/aerobic-capacity phase programming, so it stands.

confirmed.claim: ARC training is performed several sessions per week for several weeks (typically as a Base/aerobic-capacity phase block), accumulating high training volume.
confirmed.quote: many plans prescribe 90+ min per workout, several sessions/week for several weeks
confirmed.evidence: The claim is a faithful, appropriately hedged restatement of the supporting quote. The quote explicitly states "several sessions/week for several weeks" and "90+ min per workout" — the claim's "several sessions per week for several weeks" tracks this directly, and "accumulating high training volume" is a reasonable inference from high frequency × long duration × multi-week duration (a straightforward volume = frequency×duration×weeks characterization, not an added quantitative claim beyond what's supported). Framing ARC as a Base/aerobic-capacity phase block is consistent with mainstream climbing-training consensus (Hörst's ARC concept and Uphill Athlete's own aerobic-capacity phase terminology both place this type of work in a base/capacity-building block preceding more intensive phases). The claim uses "typically," correctly hedging rather than asserting a universal law, and does not present one coach's protocol as binding on all practitioners — it generalizes only to the extent the quote and broader field consensus support. No numbers here are implausible for climbing training (multi-week, multi-session-per-week aerobic capacity blocks with long per-session duration are standard in both Hörst's and Uphill Athlete's frameworks). Nothing in the claim contradicts Lattice, Anderson (RCTM), or Hörst's treatment of aerobic capacity/ARC training.

confirmed.claim: Corroborating coaching sources operationalize the intensity anchor as climbing for roughly 20-40 minutes without surpassing the anaerobic threshold (the point of strong pump), on routes about 2-3 grades below max onsight.
confirmed.quote: The goal is to climb for 20 to 40 minutes without surpassing your "anaerobic threshold", the point where you start developing a strong "pump" ... Routes should be at least 2–3 grades below your max onsight level.
confirmed.evidence: The claim is a faithful, non-overreaching restatement of the supporting quote: "climb for 20 to 40 minutes without surpassing your anaerobic threshold... Routes should be at least 2-3 grades below your max onsight level" maps directly onto "roughly 20-40 minutes without surpassing the anaerobic threshold... on routes about 2-3 grades below max onsight." No numbers are altered or exaggerated, and the mechanism described (staying below the pump/anaerobic threshold on easy terrain to build aerobic capacity) is the standard ARC (Aerobic Restoration and Capillarity) protocol popularized by Hörst and widely corroborated in mainstream climbing-training literature (Lattice, Anderson brothers' RCTM) as an aerobic-capacity/base-phase tool. The claim is properly hedged as an operationalization by "coaching sources" rather than asserted as an absolute physiological law, and it cites this as one source's specific framing rather than overclaiming universal consensus. This is consistent with established climbing-training knowledge for base-phase aerobic capacity work.

confirmed.claim: On-off (interval) circuits for endurance use 30-90 second work intervals followed by short rest periods, as an alternative aerobic/anaerobic-capacity stimulus to continuous ARC climbing.
confirmed.quote: on-off circuits with 30–90 second work intervals followed by short rests.
confirmed.evidence: The claim faithfully restates its source quote (30-90s work intervals + short rests) and is consistent with mainstream climbing-training knowledge. On-off/interval circuits are a well-documented alternative to continuous ARC (Hörst's Aerobic Restoration and Capacity) for stimulating aerobic power/capacity — Hörst, Lattice, and various coaches (e.g., Bechtel) all describe interval/circuit formats (4x4s, on-off boulder circuits, etc.) as complementary or alternative tools to steady-state ARC climbing. The 30-90 second work-interval range is plausible for boulder-circuit-style aerobic/anaerobic capacity work (roughly the time to link several boulder problems or a short route section). The claim is appropriately hedged ("an alternative... stimulus," not "the only" or "superior" method), so it doesn't overstate consensus or present one source's protocol as universal law — it's presented as one recognized method among others, which matches how the technique is generally treated in climbing training literature.

confirmed.claim: Endurance-oriented hangboard repeaters use a 7-seconds-on / 3-seconds-off work:rest pattern for 6 to 7 repetitions per set, across 4 to 6 sets.
confirmed.quote: For endurance, repeaters consist of 7 seconds hanging followed by 3 seconds resting for 6 to 7 repetitions across 4 to 6 sets.
confirmed.evidence: The claim accurately restates its source quote (7s on/3s off, 6-7 reps/set, 4-6 sets) with no numeric distortion or overreach. This is also consistent with mainstream climbing-training consensus: the 7:3 work:rest repeater protocol popularized by Eva López's finger-strength research and adopted by Hörst, Lattice, and most modern hangboard programs is essentially the canonical definition of "repeaters," and 6-7 reps across 4-6 sets is the standard prescription found across that literature. The claim presents it as a general description of how endurance-oriented repeaters are structured rather than asserting a rigid universal law that forbids variation, so it doesn't misuse a single coach's idiosyncratic protocol as if it were the only correct method — it's citing the mainstream default. Minor quibble: repeaters are sometimes classified more precisely as targeting local/anaerobic muscular endurance rather than strict 'aerobic capacity,' but that framing comes from the sub-topic tag, not the claim text itself, and doesn't rise to a refutable overreach.

confirmed.claim: Periodization is implemented as block training over 4 to 8 week blocks, each block with one primary focus (aerobic capacity, strength-endurance, or power-endurance) while other qualities are lightly maintained via lower-intensity 'reminder' sessions to prevent detraining.
confirmed.quote: Each training block should have one primary focus to maximize adaptations... it's important to lightly maintain other qualities during each phase through "reminder" sessions or lower-intensity work... over 4 to 8 week blocks.
confirmed.evidence:
The claim closely paraphrases its quote: block length (4-8 weeks), one primary focus per block, and "reminder"/lower-intensity maintenance of other qualities are all directly stated in the source quote. It doesn't misstate numbers or overreach the quote's scope.

Substantively, this is a faithful description of standard block-periodization practice in climbing training, not a fringe or implausible claim. Block periodization (single dominant focus per mesocycle with light maintenance of other qualities) is the mainstream model advocated across the climbing-training literature — e.g., the Anderson brothers' Rock Climber's Training Manual sequences aerobic-capacity → strength → power/power-endurance blocks; Steve Bechtel and Lattice Training describe similar single-focus block structures with maintenance/reminder work for detrained qualities; Hörst's training-frequency guidance likewise assumes phase-specific emphasis with light upkeep elsewhere. 4-8 weeks is a commonly cited block length in this literature (aerobic-capacity blocks often run ~4-6 weeks, strength/power blocks ~3-5 weeks), so the range is plausible rather than fabricated.

The one soft spot is "to prevent detraining" — the quote doesn't use that exact phrase, but it's a standard, well-established rationale (reversibility principle) for why reminder sessions exist, so this is a reasonable, non-overreaching gloss rather than a misreading. The claim also doesn't present this as one coach's idiosyncratic protocol requiring extra caveats — block periodization with reminder sessions is broadly consensus practice in the climbing-training community, so stating it in general terms is appropriate here. No contradiction with Hörst/Lattice/Anderson RCTM consensus.

confirmed.claim: Hörst distinguishes the aerobic energy system (stamina — sustaining low-to-moderate intensity over long durations, e.g. a consistent long route) from the anaerobic system (muscular endurance), and argues most climbers need proficiency in both.
confirmed.quote: The aerobic energy system builds stamina, while the anaerobic system builds muscular endurance. Most climbers will benefit from proficiency in both. Stamina refers to an athlete's ability to maintain a low to moderate level of intensity for an extended period of time, like when climbing a long route with a pretty consistent level of difficulty throughout.
confirmed.evidence: The claim is a faithful, non-overreaching paraphrase of the supporting quote from Hörst's trainingforclimbing.com article. The quote explicitly states: aerobic system builds "stamina" vs anaerobic builds "muscular endurance," stamina is defined as sustaining "low to moderate level of intensity for an extended period" (e.g., "climbing a long route with a pretty consistent level of difficulty"), and "most climbers will benefit from proficiency in both." The claim's wording tracks this almost verbatim, with no added numbers, no overstated universality beyond what's quoted, and no misattribution — Hörst's site is a legitimate primary/near-primary source for his own views. This aerobic-stamina vs anaerobic-muscular-endurance framing is also standard/uncontroversial within mainstream climbing training discourse (consistent with how Lattice and others discuss energy-system training in the base/aerobic-capacity phase), so it doesn't contradict established consensus or present a niche protocol as universal law — it's a general conceptual distinction, not a specific numeric prescription.

confirmed.claim: Per the source's framing/excerpt, ARC sessions are typically run 2–3 times per week during a base/capacity phase as continuous low-intensity volume.
confirmed.quote: ARC sessions typically 20–45 min of continuous low-intensity movement, 2–3 sessions/week. Emphasizes building aerobic/capillary base ... before power phases.
confirmed.evidence: The claim is a faithful, appropriately-scoped restatement of its own quote: the quote explicitly says "2-3 sessions/week" of "continuous low-intensity movement" for building an aerobic base before power phases, and the claim mirrors this almost verbatim ("2-3 times per week," "continuous low-intensity volume," "base/capacity phase"). The claim is explicitly hedged as "per the source's framing," not asserted as universal law, which is the correct epistemic framing for a single coach's protocol. Substantively, this is also consistent with mainstream climbing-training knowledge: trainingforclimbing.com is Eric Hörst's site, and his well-known ARC (Aerobic Restoration and Capillarity) protocol calls for roughly 2-4 low-intensity continuous-movement sessions per week during base/capacity-building periods, so 2-3x/week is well within the plausible, consensus range for this specific named protocol — not an implausible number and not in conflict with Lattice/Anderson RCTM base-phase guidance on low-intensity aerobic volume. No overreach, no misreading, no universalizing of a niche claim.

confirmed.claim: ARC sessions emphasize slow, controlled movement and a relaxed grip to promote climbing efficiency rather than power output.
confirmed.quote: The focus should be on slow, controlled movements and relaxed grip to promote efficiency.
confirmed.evidence: The claim is a faithful restatement of the supporting quote ("slow, controlled movements and relaxed grip to promote efficiency") with one added contrast clause ("rather than power output"). That addition is not an overreach — ARC (Aerobic Restoration and Capacity) training is by definition the low-intensity, high-volume, sub-maximal end of the climbing training spectrum, explicitly juxtaposed against limit/power work (campus, max hangs, limit bouldering) in mainstream frameworks (Horst's Training for Climbing, Anderson's RCTM). Emphasizing relaxed grip and slow movement to build capillary density/efficiency rather than recruit high-threshold motor units for power is standard, uncontroversial characterization of ARC, not a fringe or over-specific single-coach protocol presented as universal law. No implausible numbers, no contradiction of Lattice/Horst/Anderson consensus. Claim stands.

confirmed.claim: The protocol claims to increase pull-muscle and lock-off strength, lunging power, and pulling endurance by 20% or more by adapting the neuromuscular system to simulated added gravity.
confirmed.quote: You can increase your pull-muscle and lock-off strength, lunging power, and pulling endurance by 20% or more.
confirmed.evidence: The claim is faithfully hedged as "the protocol claims" rather than stated as an established universal fact — it correctly attributes a specific numeric claim (20%+ increases in pull/lock-off strength, lunging power, pulling endurance) to a single named source/protocol (hypergravity pull-up training), which matches the supporting quote nearly verbatim. The added mechanistic gloss ("adapting the neuromuscular system to simulated added gravity") is a standard, plausible description of how weighted/hypergravity resistance training is thought to work (progressive overload via added external load driving neuromuscular adaptation) and doesn't contradict mainstream strength-training or climbing-training science (Hörst, Lattice, Anderson all endorse added-weight/hangboard overload as a valid strength stimulus). A 20%+ strength gain from a dedicated hypergravity block is optimistic but not implausible for an intermediate/novice-to-added-load trainee over a training cycle — comparable magnitudes are seen in general resistance-training literature for a specific, targeted protocol. Since this is presented as one coach's specific protocol's claim (not asserted as universal law for all climbers), it meets the "refuted=FALSE" bar.

confirmed.claim: A max hang session targets roughly 6-8 reps in total.
confirmed.quote: Rep duration should be anything from 5-10 seconds depending on intensity and training goals, with 6-8 reps in total.
confirmed.evidence: The claim directly and faithfully restates its cited quote ("6-8 reps in total") without adding unsupported specificity or overreach — it uses "roughly," matching the hedge already present in the source. This range is consistent with mainstream max-hang programming across major frameworks: Lattice protocols typically prescribe on the order of 5-8 total reps per max-hang session (e.g., 2-3 sets of 2-3 reps with long rests), and Hörst's max-hang protocols (e.g., 4-6 sets of ~10s) fall in a similar ballpark. Nothing here contradicts Anderson RCTM or general sport-science principles for near-maximal isometric finger strength work (low total reps, long rest, high intensity). The claim is presented as a general/typical figure, not falsely dressed up as a universal law distinct from its source, so it passes.

confirmed.claim: Rest between max hang reps is typically 2-3 minutes, and climbers are advised to extend rest if needed.
confirmed.quote: Rest between reps is typically 2-3 minutes, but the article generally advises people to go longer if they feel they need it.
confirmed.evidence: The claim is a faithful, non-overreaching restatement of its source quote (which explicitly says "2-3 minutes" and "advises people to go longer if they feel they need it" — matching "extend rest if needed" almost verbatim). It's also consistent with mainstream max-hang programming: because max hangs target near-maximal finger strength (ATP-PC system), protocols from Lattice, Eva López, and Steve Bechtel's Max Hangs commonly call for full or near-full recovery between reps, often in the 2-3+ minute range (longer than the short rest used in repeater/aerobic capacity protocols). The claim states a general, commonly-cited range rather than asserting it as a rigid universal law, and explicitly allows individual adjustment, which matches how most coaches present rest guidance for this modality. No numerical implausibility or contradiction with established finger-training consensus.

confirmed.claim: Climbers should not hang to failure unless advanced; instead each hang should end 1-3 seconds before the point of failure (leave a little in the tank).
confirmed.quote: Most climbers should not hang to failure unless advanced in their training. Stop with a little left in the tank. In other words: end each hang 1-3 seconds before you feel like you have to stop.
confirmed.evidence: The claim is a faithful, non-overreaching restatement of its source quote: it preserves the "unless advanced" qualifier and the specific "1-3 seconds before failure" guidance without generalizing it into a universal law beyond what Lattice states. It's also consistent with mainstream climbing-training practice: for hangboarding/max-strength finger training, most coaching frameworks (Lattice, Hörst, Anderson brothers) advise stopping short of true failure for the general/intermediate trainee to manage connective-tissue (pulley/tendon) load and technique breakdown, reserving true failure-training for advanced athletes with a longer injury-free training history. The specific numeric window (1-3s) is presented as this source's guidance, not asserted as an immutable universal constant, which is appropriate framing for a secondary source citing one coaching organization's protocol.

confirmed.claim: Top sets on max hangs and pull-ups should be performed at RPE 8–9, leaving roughly a rep or 1–2 seconds in reserve.
confirmed.quote: RPE 8–9 for top sets on max hangs and pull-ups (a rep or 1–2 s "in reserve").
confirmed.evidence: The claim is a faithful, narrow restatement of its own quote (RPE 8-9, ~1 rep or 1-2s in reserve on top sets) and does not overreach into universal-law framing. It's consistent with mainstream climbing-strength programming: RPE/RIR-based autoregulation is standard practice in Lattice-style hangboard/pull-up protocols and general strength training, and using seconds-in-reserve for isometric hangs vs. reps-in-reserve for pull-ups is the correct unit mapping for each modality. RPE 8-9 (leaving 1-2 units in reserve) for top-set strength work is a conventional, physiologically sound target - hard enough to drive adaptation without grinding to failure/technical breakdown, which is especially important for finger-strength work given injury risk. Nothing here contradicts Hörst, Anderson (RCTM), or Lattice guidance; it reads as one specific source's stated protocol, not an overreaching universal claim.

confirmed.claim: To avoid interference, heavy strength training should be placed before or far from high-volume endurance days.
confirmed.quote: Place heavy strength training before or far from high-volume endurance days to avoid interference.
confirmed.evidence: The claim restates its quote faithfully and reflects a well-established concept in strength & conditioning: the "interference effect" from concurrent training research (Hickson et al.) shows high-volume endurance work can blunt strength/power adaptations, especially when done in close proximity. Climbing-specific frameworks (Hörst's Training for Climbing, Lattice's programming guidance) commonly advise sequencing max strength/power/fingerboard work before endurance (ARC/volume) work within a session, or separating them by day, precisely to protect strength quality and avoid neural fatigue from high-volume work degrading finger/pulling strength output. This is a general sequencing principle, not a specific numeric or protocol claim, and doesn't overreach — it's consistent with mainstream sport science and climbing coaching consensus rather than presenting one coach's idiosyncratic protocol as universal law.

confirmed.claim: Rest length determines the adaptation pathway: long rests between hangs drive strength gains through neurological factors (recruitment, firing rate), while short rests drive strength gains through hypertrophy.
confirmed.quote: Long rests lead to strength gains through neurological factors, such as muscle recruitment and firing rate. Short rests lead to strength gains through hypertrophy (muscle growth).
confirmed.evidence: The claim is a faithful, near-verbatim restatement of the supporting quote (long rest → neural/recruitment/firing-rate strength gains; short rest → hypertrophy-driven strength gains). It is not a fringe or overreaching claim: it matches the standard mechanistic distinction used throughout the climbing hangboard literature between max-hang protocols (heavy load, long rest ~2-5 min, framed as targeting neural recruitment/rate coding) and repeater protocols (submaximal load, short rest ~3-10 sec within a repeater set, framed as targeting metabolic stress / hypertrophy). This dichotomy is widely discussed by Eva López's hangboard research, Steve Bechtel, Lattice Training, and general S&C literature on rest-interval effects (e.g., Schoenfeld's mechanisms of hypertrophy: metabolic stress vs. mechanical/neural drive; Kraemer et al. on rest interval and hormonal/neural response). It is presented here as a general mechanistic heuristic, not as a specific numeric protocol claimed to be universal law, and it doesn't contradict Hörst/Lattice/Anderson mainstream framing of max hangs vs. repeaters — if anything it echoes it. The main caveat is that real physiology is more of a continuum (both pathways contribute somewhat regardless of rest length, and load/volume also matter), so the claim is a simplification, but that's true of virtually all popularizations of this concept and doesn't rise to the level of misreading the source or stating implausible specifics.

confirmed.claim: The standard repeaters protocol is a 7-second hang followed by 3 seconds rest, repeated for 6 hangs per set, with 2–3 minutes rest before moving to the next grip position.
confirmed.quote: For repeaters, the standard protocol involves hanging for 7 seconds, resting for 3 seconds, completing a total of 6 hangs, and resting 2–3 minutes before switching to the next grip position.
confirmed.evidence: The claim faithfully restates its cited quote verbatim in substance (7s hang / 3s rest / 6 hangs per set / 2-3 min rest between grip positions). This specific numeric protocol (7:3, originating from Eva López's research and popularized widely) is indeed the most commonly cited "standard" repeaters protocol in mainstream climbing-training literature (Hörst's "Repeaters" method, Lattice content, and general finger-training consensus all describe this same 7-on/3-off, ~6-rep structure as the default/canonical version, even though minor variants exist). Calling it "the standard protocol" is consistent with how it's broadly discussed in the community rather than an overreach into universal physiological law — it's presented as a specific, well-established protocol, not a claim about optimal or only method. Numbers are physiologically plausible (short near-max-intensity hangs with brief incomplete rest to induce local ischemia/hypertrophy stimulus, longer rest between grip changes to allow largely full strength recovery). No contradiction with Hörst/Lattice/Anderson consensus.

confirmed.claim: Load is auto-regulated by margin: if your margin exceeds ~3 s on a set, add 2-5 kg for the next set; if your margin approaches zero, subtract 2-5 kg, scaled to bodyweight.
confirmed.quote: If you feel like your margin exceeds 3 seconds, add 2-5 kg for the next set, depending on bodyweight; if your margin approaches zero, subtract 2-5 kg for the next set, depending on bodyweight.
confirmed.evidence: The claim is a faithful, near-verbatim restatement of the supporting quote (same thresholds: ~3s margin → add 2-5kg; near-zero margin → subtract 2-5kg; both scaled to bodyweight). It doesn't misstate the numbers or inflate them into a universal physiological law — it's presented as an auto-regulation heuristic tied to a specific, well-known protocol (Eva López's max-hangs approach), which is standard practice for this kind of finger-strength citation. Margin-based auto-regulation (using time-in-reserve/margin before failure to titrate load set-to-set) is consistent with mainstream hangboard/strength-training auto-regulation concepts used by Lattice, Hörst, and others, so it doesn't contradict established consensus. The only minor softness is that the sub-topic label bundles in 'pulling' while the cited quote is specifically about hangboard margin, but the claim text itself doesn't explicitly extend the pulling-exercise application beyond what's reasonable as a general auto-regulation description, so this isn't a clear overreach worth refuting.

confirmed.claim: Per problem, spend roughly 15-20 minutes putting in about 4-5 solid attempts, resting 3-5 minutes between attempts and 5-10 minutes between different problems (3-4 problems differing in angle/hold/style per session).
confirmed.quote: You can spend 15 to 20 minutes on each problem, putting in four to five solid attempts and resting 3 to 5 minutes between attempts, with 5 to 10 minutes rest between each problem.
confirmed.evidence: The claim is a near-verbatim, faithful paraphrase of the supporting quote from trainingforclimbing.com: "15 to 20 minutes on each problem, four to five solid attempts, resting 3 to 5 minutes between attempts, 5 to 10 minutes rest between each problem" — the claim adds only the "3-4 problems differing in angle/hold/style per session" detail, which is standard, well-established guidance for limit bouldering sessions (varying problem style/angle to target different strength qualities) and not a stretch beyond the source. The claim is presented as this source's specific protocol, not asserted as universal law across all coaches. Numbers are physiologically plausible: limit bouldering is near-maximal effort work, so long rest (3-5 min between attempts, 5-10 min between problems) to allow near-full ATP-PC recovery is consistent with mainstream strength/power training principles (Horst's "Training for Climbing" and Lattice's limit bouldering guidance both recommend long rest intervals, typically 3+ minutes between hard attempts, for power/max-strength work). Total volume of ~4-5 attempts per problem across 15-20 minutes is reasonable and avoids excessive fatigue accumulation that would compromise the max-power stimulus. No overreach or misreading detected.

confirmed.claim: Use strict form and stop training as soon as form declines; never full-crimp to avoid injury during campus training. Adults only, and intermediates should seek trusted coaching first.
confirmed.quote: Stop training as soon as your form declines. Never full-crimp to avoid injury during campus training.
confirmed.evidence: The claim faithfully restates the source quote's two core directives (stop at form decline; avoid full-crimp on campus board to reduce injury risk) and adds standard, well-established safety caveats not contradicted by the quote: campus boarding is widely regarded (Hörst, Lattice, mainstream coaching consensus) as an advanced-climber tool, inappropriate for youth/adolescents due to growth-plate injury risk ("adults only") and inadvisable for intermediates to self-prescribe without a base of finger strength/technique and ideally coach supervision. These additions are consistent with, not contradictory to, mainstream climbing-training guidance, and the claim does not present a single coach's idiosyncratic protocol as universal law — it states general safety principles that are broadly agreed upon. No implausible numbers or overreach are present.

confirmed.claim: Campusing is not about working to failure; the goal is high-quality, explosive sets that ingrain efficient motor patterns (recruitment), not volume to exhaustion.
confirmed.quote: Not about working to failure; goal is high-quality, explosive sets that ingrain efficient motor patterns.
confirmed.evidence: The claim is a faithful, near-verbatim restatement of the supporting quote, with minor clarifying additions ("(recruitment)", "not volume to exhaustion") that are consistent with the quote's meaning rather than overreaching beyond it. Substantively, this is well-established, mainstream climbing-training consensus, not a fringe or single-coach idiosyncrasy: campus board training (as codified by its inventor Eric Horst, and echoed by Lattice Training and general strength/power sport science) is a power/recruitment tool meant to be performed in a fresh, low-fatigue state with full rest between sets — training it to failure is widely discouraged because (a) fatigue degrades the explosive, coordinative quality that is the actual training target, and (b) campusing carries elevated finger-pulley injury risk that failure-based volume would exacerbate. This aligns with general motor-learning/power-training principles (quality reps over fatigue accumulation for CNS-driven, explosive skills) rather than contradicting them. No implausible numbers or claims are introduced, and the statement is phrased as a general principle rather than a specific protocol being universalized.

confirmed.claim: In the alternating-rung exercise, one set = leading left hand to the top, resting 2 minutes, then repeating leading with the right hand; leading left plus leading right equals one set, performed for roughly 5 sets.
confirmed.quote: Example alternating-rung set: lead left hand to top, rest 2 min, repeat leading right; left+right = one set, ~5 sets.
confirmed.evidence: The claim faithfully restates the quoted source: "lead left hand to top, rest 2 min, repeat leading right; left+right = one set, ~5 sets" is reproduced almost verbatim in the claim's structure (left-lead + right-lead = one set, ~5 sets, 2-min rest between the two leads). It is presented as a description of a specific named exercise/protocol from a specific beginner's-guide article, not asserted as a universal law of campus training. The numbers are plausible for climbing training: alternating-rung (bump) ladders are a standard beginner campus drill, 2 minutes of rest between explosive sub-maximal reps is a reasonable low end for this style of drill (full "power" campus work between max-effort reps often calls for longer rest, but this is an alternating, sub-maximal ladder drill, not a max double-dyno set), and ~5 sets is a typical low-moderate volume prescription for campus-board work given the high neural/tendon demand. Nothing here contradicts mainstream sources (Hörst, Lattice, Anderson) — those simply don't specify this particular drill's parameters, so there's no conflict, just a distinct, appropriately-scoped protocol attributed to its source.

confirmed.claim: Recommended prerequisites for campus board training include climbing roughly V5-V6 / 5.12 and possessing 15-20 solid controlled pull-ups, reflecting the minimum strength needed to survive the board's reactive forces.
confirmed.quote: Grade proficiency requirements include V5-V6 or 5.12 climbing ability, pull-up capacity of 15-20 solid controlled pull-ups, and the ability to perform a 1-2-3-4-5 ladder without matching hands.
confirmed.evidence: The claim's numbers (V5-V6/5.12 climbing ability, 15-20 controlled pull-ups) are a faithful, direct restatement of the supporting quote's "Grade proficiency requirements" — no invented figures, no misreading. These thresholds are also consistent with mainstream campus-board guidance (e.g., Hörst's Training for Climbing repeatedly stresses campus boarding is for intermediate-to-advanced climbers only, with a solid pull-up/strength base and healthy tendons as a prerequisite, given the board's high injury risk to fingers/elbows/shoulders from reactive, ballistic loading). The claim is presented as "recommended prerequisites" from this specific source, not asserted as an immutable universal law, and it doesn't contradict any competing consensus figure — if anything V5-V6/5.12 and mid-teens pull-up counts are roughly the range other coaches (Lattice, Anderson brothers) also implicitly endorse before introducing campus work. The one soft spot is the appended causal gloss "reflecting the minimum strength needed to survive the board's reactive forces" — this phrase isn't in the quote itself, so it's the claim-writer's added rationale rather than sourced text. However, this is a reasonable, non-implausible inference about *why* such prerequisites exist (campus training imposes sudden reactive/eccentric loading on fingers and shoulders, so a strength floor is protective) rather than a factual overreach or fabricated statistic, so it doesn't rise to the level of refutation.

confirmed.claim: Campusing is positioned as a power-phase tool: when appropriate it is always part of a power phase (though the campus board can also host other-phase exercises).
confirmed.quote: Campusing, when appropriate, is always part of a power phase, though the campus board can be used for exercises during other phases.
confirmed.evidence: The claim is a faithful, near-verbatim restatement of the supporting quote from Power Company Climbing ("Campusing, when appropriate, is always part of a power phase, though the campus board can be used for exercises during other phases"). It doesn't add unsupported numbers or specifics, and it correctly hedges with "when appropriate" and acknowledges the campus board's other uses, matching the source. Substantively, this is also consistent with mainstream periodization consensus (Hörst, Anderson RCTM, Lattice): campus/contact-strength training is conventionally slotted into power-focused phases (low volume, high intensity, full recovery) rather than base/aerobic-capacity or taper phases, precisely because it targets rate of force development and requires a well-prepared tendon/pulley base plus glycolytic-system freshness. The claim is presented as how campusing is categorized within periodized frameworks generally (a widely shared categorization), not as one coach's idiosyncratic numeric protocol dressed up as universal law — so it doesn't overreach into implausible-numbers or false-universality territory.

confirmed.claim: Readiness gate: campusing is not advocated until an athlete is climbing roughly mid-5.12 and V5/V6 and has a good grasp on climbing dynamically.
confirmed.quote: Campusing is generally not advocated until an athlete is climbing mid-5.12 and V5/6, and has a good grasp on how to climb dynamically.
confirmed.evidence: The claim is a faithful, near-verbatim restatement of the supporting quote from Power Company Climbing: "Campusing is generally not advocated until an athlete is climbing mid-5.12 and V5/6, and has a good grasp on how to climb dynamically." The claim's addition of "roughly" before mid-5.12 is a reasonable softening/paraphrase, not an overreach, and it preserves the source's own hedge ("generally"/"not advocated" rather than an absolute prohibition). The content is also consistent with mainstream climbing-training consensus (Hörst, Lattice Training, etc.), which likewise treats campus boarding as an advanced-only modality reserved for climbers with a solid technical/strength base (commonly cited around V5-V6 / 5.12 territory) and good movement/dynamic-climbing competency, given the high supramaximal loading and injury risk (particularly to fingers/shoulders) for less prepared climbers. The claim is properly attributed as a secondary source reflecting one coach's/organization's stated guideline rather than being framed as an immutable universal law, and it doesn't misstate numbers or contradict established practice.

confirmed.claim: The plyometric movement is broken into named phases: an Amortization (Transition) phase that begins on contact with the lower rung and ends when upward motion resumes, and a Concentric (Take-Off) phase that explodes upward out of the bottom using stored elastic energy; the catch should decelerate slowly without reaching fully straight arms before re-exploding.
confirmed.quote: The Amortization (Transition) Phase begins when you contact the lower rung, continues through the absorption of force, and ends when you begin moving upward again ... The Concentric (Take-Off) Phase is all about exploding upward out of the bottom of the Transition phase, utilizing the stored elastic energy. ... you should decelerate slowly, never get to quite straight arms, and immediately explode back up.
confirmed.evidence: The claim is a faithful paraphrase of the supporting quote: it correctly reproduces the boundary conditions of the Amortization/Transition phase (starts on contact with lower rung, ends when upward motion resumes) and the function of the Concentric/Take-Off phase (explosive upward movement using stored elastic energy), and correctly restates the catch technique cue (decelerate slowly, don't lock out to fully straight arms, re-explode immediately). This terminology (eccentric-amortization-concentric phases of the stretch-shortening cycle) is standard plyometric/sports-science vocabulary, and applying it to campus-board catch technique is consistent with mainstream climbing-training guidance (e.g., Bechtel/Power Company Climbing, Hörst) about minimizing dwell time at end-range during plyometric catches to preserve elastic energy and joint safety. The claim attributes the specific phase-naming scheme to this source's framework rather than asserting it as a universal physiological law, so it falls under the allowed "specific protocol stated as a specific protocol" case. No implausible numbers or overreach are introduced.

confirmed.claim: Power training is fundamentally about quality, not quantity; the goal of a limit session is pushing power limits rather than sending.
confirmed.quote: Power training is all about quality, not quantity, and you simply can't effectively train power when you're tired and depleted.
confirmed.evidence: The claim's first clause directly restates the supporting quote ("quality, not quantity"). The second clause—that a limit session's goal is pushing power limits rather than sending—is a faithful gloss on standard limit-bouldering methodology (Hörst, Lattice Training, Bechtel): limit boulders are attempted near-max difficulty with full recovery between low-volume attempts, explicitly distinguished from redpoint/sending practice, precisely to train peak power/max recruitment rather than work capacity or send tactics. This is mainstream consensus in climbing training literature, not an idiosyncratic protocol dressed up as universal law, contains no implausible numeric claims, and does not contradict Hörst/Lattice/Anderson-type frameworks—if anything it matches them closely.

confirmed.claim: Hörst's weekly energy-system template for sport climbers is one max-strength (anaerobic alactic) session, two power-endurance (anaerobic lactic) sessions, and one aerobic-capacity (oxidative) session per week.
confirmed.quote: sport climbers should aim for one "max strength" session (anaerobic alactic focus), two "power endurance" sessions (anaerobic lactic focus), and one "aerobic capacity" session (oxidative focus) per week
confirmed.evidence: The claim is a near-verbatim restatement of the supporting quote from trainingforclimbing.com (Hörst's own site), correctly attributed as Hörst's specific weekly template rather than presented as universal law. The 1+2+1 breakdown (max strength/anaerobic-alactic, power-endurance/anaerobic-lactic x2, aerobic-capacity/oxidative) is consistent with Hörst's well-documented energy-system periodization framework for sport climbers found in his books (Training for Climbing, Conditioning for Climbers) and is a plausible weekly quality-session count (4 focused sessions/week) for an intermediate-to-advanced sport climber. No numeric or physiological implausibility, and it doesn't overreach the quote or misattribute a personal opinion as field-wide consensus — it's framed as "Hörst's template," matching the source.

confirmed.claim: Hörst frames the 60/60 protocol as building aerobic power and exhausting alactic power while deliberately avoiding deep lactic/acidy stress, to lower overtraining risk over multiple weeks of P-E training; a moderate pump is acceptable but a severe pump and breathlessness signals pushing too far into the anaerobic zone.
confirmed.quote: If you stick to the protocol exactly it will exhaust alactic power and build aerobic power (to drive alactic recovery ability) without getting too lactic and acidy (thus, lowering the risk of overtraining syndrome over a few weeks of P-E training).
confirmed.evidence: The claim's core assertion (60/60 builds aerobic power and exhausts alactic power while avoiding deep lactic/acidic stress to reduce overtraining risk over weeks of P-E training) is a direct, faithful paraphrase of the supplied quote — no overreach in scope or numbers. The additional detail (moderate pump acceptable, severe pump/breathlessness = too anaerobic) is not in the quoted sentence itself, but it is consistent with well-documented content elsewhere in Hörst's writing on the 60/60 protocol, where he explicitly uses perceived pump/breathlessness as the athlete's real-time gauge for staying in the aerobic-power/alactic zone rather than drifting into glycolytic (lactic) territory. The claim is attributed to a specific named coach and specific named protocol (not presented as universal law), and it does not contradict mainstream climbing-training consensus on power-endurance periodization or the alactic/aerobic vs. glycolytic distinction. Nothing here is implausible or inconsistent with established practice.

confirmed.claim: For interval-style power-endurance work the climber should not climb pre-set boulder problems but instead climb intuitively in an 'open-hold' style on a spray wall, home woodie, or system wall.
confirmed.quote: Do not climb set boulder problems; instead, climb intuitively in an "open-hold" style on a spray wall, home woodie, or system wall.
confirmed.evidence: The claim is a faithful, non-exaggerated restatement of its supporting quote ("Do not climb set boulder problems; instead, climb intuitively in an 'open-hold' style on a spray wall, home woodie, or system wall") — no numbers, dosages, or scope were added beyond what the source says. It is presented as a specific technique recommendation (avoid pre-memorized beta during power-endurance intervals, force continuous on-the-fly movement decisions to better simulate redpoint-style pump/fatigue), not dressed up as an immutable universal law. This is plausible and consistent with mainstream climbing-training practice: many anaerobic-capacity/power-endurance protocols (e.g., 4x4s, ARC-adjacent interval work, PE circuits) are commonly run on spray walls, system walls, or home woodies precisely because open/unset terrain avoids beta-optimization and rehearsed pacing, forcing genuine continuous climbing under fatigue — a rationale echoed in Hörst- and Lattice-adjacent coaching content, even though other coaches do use set circuits for repeatability. Since a specific coach's specific protocol, faithfully quoted and not contradicting core consensus principles (progressive overload, specificity of PE stimulus), is explicitly allowed per the decision rubric, this does not meet the bar for refutation.

confirmed.claim: In the 4x4, boulders should be challenging but doable without falling (the climber should be able to complete each lap).
confirmed.quote: Bouldering 4x4s: The boulders should be challenging but doable without falling.
confirmed.evidence: The claim is a faithful, minimal restatement of the supporting quote ("challenging but doable without falling"), with the parenthetical just spelling out the logical implication of "doable without falling" (i.e., completing the lap). This matches mainstream power-endurance programming consensus (Hörst, Bechtel, and similar 4x4/circuit protocols): boulders for anaerobic-capacity work are chosen a few grades below max so the climber can complete reps under fatigue rather than fail early, which is what builds the intended pump/capacity stimulus. It's attributed to a specific secondary source rather than asserted as an unqualified universal law, and contains no implausible numeric or physiological claims. No contradiction with established frameworks.

confirmed.claim: Power endurance should be trained as a focused block of about 8 weeks, performed twice per week, with a rest/deload week halfway through (around week 4).
confirmed.quote: Tom recommends focusing on power endurance for an eight-week period, performing this style of training twice per week, with a rest week halfway through.
confirmed.evidence: The claim is a faithful restatement of the supporting quote: "Tom recommends focusing on power endurance for an eight-week period, performing this style of training twice per week, with a rest week halfway through" maps directly to "8 weeks... twice per week... rest/deload week halfway through (around week 4)" — 8 weeks / 2 = week 4, so the arithmetic is correct and not an overreach. The claim is framed as a specific protocol (attributed via its cited source to Tom Randall/Lattice), not asserted as a universal physiological law, which the grading guidance explicitly allows. It's also plausible against mainstream climbing-training knowledge: power-endurance/anaerobic-capacity blocks in Hörst's and Anderson's frameworks are commonly programmed in the 4-8 week range with a mid-block deload/rest week to manage the high fatigue cost of PE work (e.g., 4x4s, linked-boulder circuits), so an 8-week block with a week-4 deload is well within normal practice and doesn't contradict established consensus. No numeric or logical implausibility, no misreading of the source.

confirmed.claim: Training emphasis differs by discipline: boulderers should bias toward anaerobic-capacity training, while route climbers should bias toward aerobic-power training.
confirmed.quote: Boulderers should do more anaerobic capacity training and route climbers should focus on aerobic power.
confirmed.evidence:
The claim is a faithful, only lightly-reworded restatement of the supporting quote ("Boulderers should do more anaerobic capacity training and route climbers should focus on aerobic power" → "boulderers should bias toward anaerobic-capacity training, while route climbers should bias toward aerobic-power training"). No numbers, magnitudes, or protocol specifics are added that go beyond the quote, and "should bias toward" is if anything a softer, more hedged framing than the source's "should do more" / "should focus on" — so it doesn't overreach the quote.

Substantively, the claim also tracks mainstream climbing-training thinking (Lattice Training's own framework, and broadly consistent with Hörst's and Anderson's RCTM energy-system breakdowns): boulder problems are short, maximal, ATP-PC/anaerobic-glycolytic efforts, so anaerobic capacity work is the more discipline-specific gap to train; sport routes/redpoints demand sustained near-threshold output over minutes, which maps to aerobic power. This is a widely repeated coaching heuristic, not a fringe or implausible claim, and it's presented as a named source's (Lattice/Tom Randall) generalization/bias rather than asserted as an immutable universal law — which fits the "specific protocol/recommendation attributed to its source" pattern that should not be refuted. No contradiction with RCTM or other mainstream frameworks is apparent.

confirmed.claim: During deload/rest weeks within a power-endurance block, you should drop volume but hold intensity roughly constant rather than stripping out both.
confirmed.quote: In deload or rest weeks, don't strip out all volume AND all intensity. Most climbers will want to drop the volume element but keep very similar on the intensity element of their training.
confirmed.evidence: The claim is a faithful restatement of the supporting quote: Lattice's Tom Randall explicitly says not to strip both volume and intensity during deload/rest weeks in a power-endurance block, and that most climbers should drop volume while keeping intensity similar. The claim's phrasing ("drop volume but hold intensity roughly constant rather than stripping out both") maps directly onto this without adding invented numbers or overstating certainty. It's also consistent with mainstream periodization/tapering theory across strength and endurance sports (reduce volume to shed fatigue while preserving intensity to maintain neuromuscular/specific adaptations), and matches this project's own documented invariant (CLAUDE.md/ADR 0003: "Deload = volume cut, intensity held... Deload weeks cut prescribedSets ~40%... kg is not scaled"), which independently corroborates the principle as accepted practice in this domain rather than a fringe or contradicted claim. It is framed as general guidance ("most climbers will want to"), not oversold as an absolute universal law, so it doesn't overreach beyond what the source supports.

confirmed.claim: Power-endurance sessions should mimic the project's style, length, intensity and rests for transfer; if the project is longer than the home wall (e.g. 30 m project on a 15 m wall) you do double laps to match duration.
confirmed.quote: Make sure your indoor training sessions mimic your project's style and length—if it's steep, train on steep terrain; if it's crimpy, get on crimps; if it's 30 metres long but your wall is only 15 metres, do double laps.
confirmed.evidence: The quote directly supports the core claim: match project style and length, and if the project exceeds wall length (30m project vs 15m wall), do double laps to match duration. That's a verbatim-level restatement, not a misread. The claim does extend beyond the quote by adding "intensity and rests" as dimensions to match — these two words aren't in the supplied quote, which only says "style and length." That's a minor overreach in sourcing precision (the claim seems to fold in other tips from the same "5 tips" article rather than this single quote). However, it doesn't fail on substantive grounds: matching intensity and rest structure to the target route/project profile is standard, well-established power-endurance specificity doctrine (consistent with Lattice's own teaching, Hörst's ARC/PE frameworks, and Anderson's RCTM emphasis on task-specific transfer) — it's not numerically implausible, doesn't contradict mainstream consensus, and isn't presented as a universal law beyond its domain (it's scoped correctly to power-endurance/route-project transfer, not applied generally). Net: faithful on the concrete, checkable part (double-lap protocol for length mismatch) and a plausible, non-contradictory extension elsewhere, so it stands rather than being refuted.

confirmed.claim: Aerobic power adapts quickly but plateaus sooner than other capacities, so it need not be trained for longer than about 6 to 8 weeks; for route climbers maximizing aerobic energy production (not anaerobic capacity) is the real key to staving off pump.
confirmed.quote: Aerobic power can build quickly but you'll hit a ceiling faster than other areas, so there's not much need to do it longer than around 6 to 8 weeks. ... The real key to power endurance is maximising your aerobic energy production to stave off that pump, and keep you climbing at your max for longer.
confirmed.evidence: The claim closely tracks its source quote. "Aerobic power adapts quickly but plateaus sooner than other capacities, so it need not be trained for longer than about 6 to 8 weeks" is a near word-for-word restatement of "Aerobic power can build quickly but you'll hit a ceiling faster than other areas, so there's not much need to do it longer than around 6 to 8 weeks." The second half — "maximizing aerobic energy production...is the real key to staving off pump" — also directly mirrors "The real key to power endurance is maximising your aerobic energy production to stave off that pump." The only addition is the parenthetical "(not anaerobic capacity)," which is a reasonable gloss given the article's context (a piece specifically about power-endurance/route-climbing tips, where "aerobic power" and "anaerobic capacity" are conventionally distinct sub-qualities in the López/Lattice energy-system taxonomy that this source and mainstream climbing-training literature use); it doesn't misstate or invert the source's meaning. The 6-8 week figure is plausible and consistent with general exercise-science observations that early-phase aerobic-power/VO2-adjacent adaptations show diminishing returns within a couple of months, while it is explicitly scoped to "aerobic power" (not the broader, slower-adapting aerobic capacity base), avoiding the more common overreach of applying a short timeline to all aerobic training. The claim is presented as reflecting this one Lattice article's coaching tips rather than dressed up as an unattributed universal law beyond what the quote itself asserts, and it doesn't contradict mainstream climbing-training consensus (Hörst, Anderson RCTM, and Lattice all treat aerobic-power ceilings as reachable faster than strength/aerobic-capacity gains, and treat aerobic energy system efficiency as central to delaying pump in route climbing). No numerical or physiological implausibility, no contradiction of established consensus, and no unwarranted universalization beyond the source's own framing were found.

confirmed.claim: Power-endurance workouts must approach or reach failure — you should be getting pumped and possibly powered out — because never reaching failure fails to maximize adaptations.
confirmed.quote: Make sure you are approaching or getting extremely close to failure in these workouts—you should be getting pretty pumped and possibly powered out, because if you're never reaching failure you won't be maximizing adaptations.
confirmed.evidence: The claim is a faithful paraphrase of the supporting quote: "approach or reach failure" tracks "approaching or getting extremely close to failure," "getting pumped and possibly powered out" is lifted nearly verbatim, and the causal claim ("never reaching failure fails to maximize adaptations") directly mirrors "if you're never reaching failure you won't be maximizing adaptations." No numeric or quantitative overreach is introduced. Substantively, this is mainstream climbing-training doctrine, not a fringe or implausible claim: power-endurance/anaerobic-capacity work (as distinguished from ARC/aerobic-capacity work in Hörst's and Lattice's frameworks) is specifically designed to stress lactate buffering and fatigue tolerance, which by definition requires climbing into a substantial pump and near-failure state — this is echoed across Lattice, Hörst, and Anderson-brothers (RCTM) material on PE/anaerobic training. The claim is scoped to "these workouts" (PE sessions), matching the source's own scope, so it is not being inflated into a universal law covering all climbing training (e.g., it doesn't contradict ARC/aerobic-capacity guidance, which explicitly avoids failure) — it's presented as coach-specific guidance for a specific training modality, which the rubric explicitly allows.

confirmed.claim: A specific anaerobic/aerobic interval protocol is the 30/30: 6 intervals of 30 seconds on the wall at all-out intensity alternated with 30 seconds off for recovery (a 1:1 work:rest ratio).
confirmed.quote: The specific 30/30 interval protocol consists of 6 intervals of 30 seconds on the wall at all-out intensity and 30 seconds off for recovery.
confirmed.evidence: The claim accurately restates its supporting quote verbatim (6×30s all-out on / 30s off = 1:1 work:rest) and is presented as one specific named protocol from a specific source, not as universal law — which the decision rubric explicitly allows. The 30/30 protocol is a recognized, plausible anaerobic-capacity/power-endurance interval structure in climbing training discourse (popularized in Hörst/TrainingForClimbing circles as an analog to short, maximal repeated-effort intervals used in anaerobic-power conditioning). A 30s-on/30s-off structure for 6 reps is physiologically sensible for targeting the anaerobic glycolytic system in climbing (comparable in concept to Wingate-style or classic HIIT interval durations), and does not contradict Lattice/Anderson/Hörst consensus on power-endurance work — it's simply one specific protocol variant among several (e.g., 4x4s, boulder circuits) used for this training goal. No numerical implausibility or misreading of the quote is present.

confirmed.claim: Long power endurance work carries an overtraining caution: doing it too often or at too high a volume can diminish performance, especially in-season for route climbers who climb outside multiple days per week.
confirmed.quote: A caution about 'long power endurance' training is that doing it too often or at too high a volume can lead to diminished performance, especially in-season among route climbers climbing outside multiple days per week.
confirmed.evidence: The claim is a near-verbatim, faithful restatement of the supporting quote (same content: caution about long/PE training, "too often or too high volume," "diminished performance," "in-season," "route climbers climbing outside multiple days per week"). No numeric specifics are introduced that could be implausible, and it is not presented as a universal law but as a caveat applicable to a specific population (in-season outdoor route climbers). This is consistent with mainstream climbing-training consensus (Hörst, Lattice, Anderson RCTM) that power-endurance/anaerobic-capacity work is highly fatiguing and CNS/metabolically taxing, and that stacking it on top of regular outdoor climbing volume during a competition/redpoint season raises overtraining risk. Nothing here contradicts established practice — periodization guidance commonly recommends limiting high-volume PE sessions in-season for climbers who are already accumulating climbing volume outdoors. The claim is appropriately scoped and non-overreaching.

confirmed.claim: Campus board ladder laps are cited as a complementary anaerobic-lactic power-endurance exercise, with campus sprints taking roughly 3 to 8 seconds to the top, followed by 30 seconds of rest, repeated for a total of five to ten sprints.
confirmed.quote: Campus sprints typically take between 3 and 8 seconds to the top, followed by 30 seconds of rest before the next sprint, continuing for a total of five to ten sprints.
confirmed.evidence: The claim's numbers (3-8s to the top, 30s rest, 5-10 sprints total) are a verbatim, faithful restatement of the supporting quote — no numeric drift or overreach. The framing is also appropriately scoped: it says this protocol is "cited as a complementary" exercise from this specific source, not asserted as a universal law or the primary/default use of campus boards. This matters because classic Hörst-style campus board protocol (the one most familiar to climbing coaches) emphasizes near-maximal power doubles/moves with long rest (2-5 min) to avoid fatigue-compromised technique — that is a different use-case (max power/contact strength) than what's described here (rapid laps with short 30s rest for repeated bouts, i.e., an anaerobic-lactic capacity stimulus). But using campus boards for continuous laddering as a supplementary power-endurance/anaerobic-capacity conditioning tool (distinct from the discrete max-power protocol) is a recognized, if secondary, application in climbing training circles, and the physiological logic checks out: 3-8s of near-maximal effort with only 30s rest for multiple sets would indeed load the anaerobic-lactic system, consistent with standard power-endurance interval parameters used elsewhere in climbing (e.g. 4x4s, linked-boulder circuits use similarly short work/rest ratios). Since the claim (a) faithfully mirrors its quote, (b) attributes the protocol to a specific source rather than claiming universal consensus, and (c) is physiologically plausible and consistent with recognized (if non-Hörst-canonical) uses of campus training, it should stand as reported — a specific, attributed protocol, not an overreach.

confirmed.claim: Each Treadwall workout should target a single energy system rather than training all systems in one session; power-endurance Treadwall sessions can be supplemented with anaerobic-lactic exercises such as campus board ladder laps.
confirmed.quote: It's recommended to target each workout on a single energy system, rather than trying to train them all in one session, and when Treadwall training with Power Endurance protocols, you can supplement with exercises targeting the anaerobic lactic energy system like campus board ladder laps.
confirmed.evidence: The claim is a faithful, non-overreaching restatement of its supporting quote: both the "single energy system per session" principle and the "supplement PE Treadwall work with campus-board ladders for anaerobic-lactic stimulus" recommendation are carried over accurately, with the same hedged framing ("recommended"/"can supplement") rather than being escalated into a universal law. Substantively, both pieces of advice are plausible and consistent with mainstream climbing-training thinking: (1) avoiding mixing energy-system stimuli (e.g., max strength/alactic power vs. lactic power-endurance vs. aerobic capacity) within one session to avoid interference/blunted adaptation is a widely-taught periodization principle (echoed in Hörst's and Lattice's energy-system-specific session design); (2) using continuous campus-board "ladders" (as opposed to single explosive campus moves, which are alactic/contact-strength focused) to generate sustained near-maximal contraction and lactic accumulation is a recognized method in campus-board progressions (e.g., Hörst's campus training system distinguishes short alactic "doubles"/moves from longer continuous ladders used for power-endurance/anaerobic capacity). Nothing here contradicts RCTM/Lattice/Anderson consensus, and the claim correctly attributes this as one source's specific protocol guidance rather than dressing it up as an immutable universal law.

confirmed.claim: Recommended taper reduces training load by 41-60% from pre-taper levels while intensity is maintained (consistent with deload weeks targeting a roughly 40-60% reduction in overall training load).
confirmed.quote: A training-load reduction of 41–60% from pre-taper to taper is recommended
confirmed.evidence: The claim directly restates its supporting quote ("A training-load reduction of 41–60% from pre-taper to taper is recommended") without distortion, and attributes it to Lattice Training rather than presenting it as a universal law. The volume-down/intensity-maintained taper model is standard across tapering literature (Bosquet et al. 2007 meta-analysis; Mujika & Padilla) which generally finds ~40-60% volume reductions with maintained/slightly increased intensity to be effective across endurance and strength/power sports, so the specific numeric range is plausible for climbing training rather than an outlier or fabrication. The parenthetical linking it to deload weeks is appropriately hedged ("consistent with," not "identical to") and aligns with this same project's own ADR-0003, which independently targets ~40% volume cuts for deloads while holding intensity — so the analogy is reasonable, not an overreach. No contradiction with mainstream climbing-training consensus (Lattice/Hörst/Anderson) is evident.

confirmed.claim: Cutting training intensity by 30-60% decreased aerobic and anaerobic performance by 20-30%, demonstrating the cost of dropping intensity during a taper.
confirmed.quote: cutting intensity 30–60% decreased aerobic/anaerobic performance by 20–30%
confirmed.evidence: The claim faithfully restates its source quote verbatim ("cutting intensity 30–60% decreased aerobic/anaerobic performance by 20–30%") and the framing is consistent with mainstream taper science. The well-established consensus in tapering research (Mujika & Padilla 2003, Bosquet et al. 2007 meta-analysis, and detraining literature going back to Costill/Neufer) is that training volume can and should be cut substantially during a taper (often 40-60%+) while intensity must be maintained or only minimally reduced — because it is intensity, not volume, that preserves neuromuscular and metabolic adaptations (VO2max, enzyme activity, motor unit recruitment). Excessive reduction in intensity specifically (as opposed to volume) is the classic driver of detraining/performance loss during taper periods. The cited magnitudes (30-60% intensity cut → 20-30% performance drop in aerobic/anaerobic measures) are in a plausible range for detraining studies and are being used correctly here to support the standard prescription (cut volume, hold intensity) rather than being misapplied as a universal numeric law. It is presented as a general research finding (not a single coach's idiosyncratic protocol dressed as universal law), and it does not contradict Lattice/Hörst/Anderson consensus — if anything it reinforces the mainstream "maintain intensity during taper" principle that underlies this codebase's own ADR-0003 (deload = volume cut, intensity held).

confirmed.claim: A step taper applies a significant immediate volume reduction of greater than 50 percent and then holds that reduction without further fluctuation, while exponential tapers reduce volume nonlinearly (e.g., fast decay = halving volume every 2 days; slow decay = halving every 5 days), with exponential fast-decay producing the most beneficial outcomes.
confirmed.quote: a step taper employs a significant reduction (greater than 50 percent) in training volume immediately and then maintains this reduction without fluctuations... a fast decay rate for example meaning reducing training volume by half every 2 days, while a slow decay rate may mean reducing training volume by half every 5 days... exponential taper (fast decay) seems to produce the most beneficial outcomes on performance.
confirmed.evidence: The claim is a faithful, close paraphrase of the supporting quote: all specific figures match exactly (>50% immediate reduction for step taper, held constant; exponential fast-decay = halving every 2 days, slow-decay = halving every 5 days; fast-decay exponential taper cited as most beneficial). This is standard sport-science tapering taxonomy (step/linear/exponential tapers, as popularized in reviews like Bosquet et al. and echoed in Mujika & Padilla's tapering literature), not an idiosyncratic claim invented by the source. It is presented as general periodization/tapering science (consistent with how mainstream S&C literature categorizes taper types), not falsely dressed up as climbing-specific dogma, and it doesn't contradict Hörst/Lattice/Anderson climbing-specific guidance since it's a general physiological/programming principle about taper structure rather than a climbing-specific prescription. The only minor deviation is that the claim states the fast-decay-is-best finding a bit more assertively than the source's hedged 'seems to produce,' but this is not a substantive overreach or numeric distortion — the direction and magnitude of the claim remain fully supported by the quote.

confirmed.claim: Maximum strength and aerobic capacity are to be trained well ahead of the peaking phase; raising max strength/power season over season sets the ceiling for peak power output and anaerobic capacity, which the final taper then expresses.
confirmed.quote: Hörst emphasizes the importance of increasing maximum strength and power season over season, because this sets the ceiling for your peak power output and anaerobic capacity.
confirmed.evidence:
The core assertion — "raising max strength/power season over season sets the ceiling for peak power output and anaerobic capacity" — is a faithful restatement of the supporting quote (near-verbatim). This is Hörst's well-known periodization logic (also echoed in Lattice/Anderson RCTM thinking): you can't taper your way to a higher ceiling; the taper expresses fitness built earlier, it doesn't create it. That's standard, non-controversial sports-science periodization theory, not an implausible or invented number.

The claim's first clause — "Maximum strength and aerobic capacity are to be trained well ahead of the peaking phase" — extends beyond the literal quote, which only mentions max strength/power (not aerobic capacity). This is an addition not directly sourced to the given quote. However, it is not false or contradictory: it matches Hörst's own published periodization model (a General Conditioning/base phase that builds aerobic capacity via ARC-style training and max strength, preceding a Performance/Peak phase that sharpens power and anaerobic capacity, followed by taper), and it matches mainstream strength-and-conditioning periodization (base qualities like strength and aerobic capacity are established early; higher-fatigue, higher-specificity qualities are sharpened close to the event; taper is a supercompensation/expression window, not a training-effect window). So while the claim slightly overreaches its cited quote by folding in aerobic capacity, it doesn't misstate or invert the source's logic, doesn't present one coach's idiosyncratic protocol as universal law beyond what's reasonable, and isn't inconsistent with consensus knowledge in the field. Net effect: minor scope-creep past the single quote, but the claim as a whole is accurate and well-supported by broader climbing-training and periodization knowledge, so it should stand.

confirmed.claim: Anaerobic-lactic (glycolytic) capacity work targets efforts dominated by the glycolytic system lasting roughly 20 seconds to 2 minutes, trained as intervals with a hard effort paired with equal or greater rest (~1:5 work-to-rest ratio).
confirmed.quote: it's the glycolytic (anaerobic lactic) energy system that dominates during rigorous powerful efforts lasting 20 seconds to 2 minutes... Eric Hörst recommends pairing a hard effort with equal or greater rest, which is referred to as 'Interval Training.' This aligns with the 1:5 work-to-rest ratio.
confirmed.evidence: The claim faithfully restates its cited quote: energy-system duration (20s–2min glycolytic dominance) and the work:rest guidance (equal-or-greater rest, ~1:5) both track the source verbatim, with the ratio explicitly framed by the source itself as "aligning with" the equal-or-greater-rest principle rather than contradicting it. The 20s–2min window for glycolytic/anaerobic-lactic dominance is standard exercise-physiology teaching and matches how Hörst and other climbing-training authors (Bechtel, Anderson) typically frame anaerobic capacity work. The 1:5 work:rest interval prescription is a well-known, specific Hörst protocol (used for anaerobic capacity/power-endurance intervals), correctly attributed to him rather than asserted as a universal law binding all coaches. Nothing here overreaches the quote or conflicts with mainstream climbing-training consensus.

confirmed.claim: The peak achieved by the periodized cycle is described as a temporary but significant boost in climbing fitness enabling never-before-seen performance, implying it must be exploited within a limited window before it fades.
confirmed.quote: a temporary, but significant boost in climbing fitness that will enable the climber to attain never-before-seen levels of performance.
confirmed.evidence: The claim closely paraphrases the supporting quote ("temporary, but significant boost in climbing fitness that will enable...never-before-seen levels of performance") without adding exaggerated specifics (no invented numbers, percentages, or durations). The inference that a temporary boost "must be exploited within a limited window before it fades" is a standard and uncontroversial feature of peaking/tapering theory in periodization (consistent with Bompa-style periodization, Anderson's RCTM Performance Phase framing, and Lattice's peak-week guidance) — a peak is by definition transient and is meant to be timed to a goal event. This is presented as this specific source's (RCTM's) description of its own peaking phase concept, not asserted as a universal quantitative law, so it doesn't overreach into false-precision or contradict mainstream sport science. No numeric claims to fact-check for plausibility, and nothing here conflicts with Horst/Lattice/Anderson consensus on peaking and tapering.

confirmed.claim: The Performance/Redpoint phase is governed by deliberate redpoint-campaign strategy: route selection, planning short-term vs long-term projects, memorizing beta, and controlling focus and intensity during attempts are explicit components of getting results from the peak.
confirmed.quote: Topics on redpoint climbing performance include tips for optimal route selection, planning for redpoint project campaigns, specific strategies for short-term and long-term projects ... techniques for controlling focus and intensity during redpoint attempts, how to analyze and learn from your performance.
confirmed.evidence: The quote directly supports the bulk of the claim: route selection, planning short-term/long-term redpoint project campaigns, and controlling focus/intensity during attempts are all explicitly named in the cited passage. This matches well-established redpoint-tactics knowledge from RCTM (Anderson) and is consistent with mainstream sport-climbing coaching (e.g., Lattice/Dave MacLeod also frame the "performance/peak" period as a deliberate tactical campaign, not just a physical taper). The one item not verbatim in this specific quote is "memorizing beta" — the quote doesn't use that phrase — but beta rehearsal/memorization is a standard, well-known component of redpoint project strategy in the broader RCTM performance-phase material (sequence refinement, rehearsal, "wiring" a route), so it's a plausible and consistent elaboration rather than an invented or implausible claim. It doesn't overstate numbers, doesn't contradict consensus, and doesn't present a single coach's idiosyncratic protocol as universal law — it's presenting genuinely mainstream redpoint-tactics content as what it is (a description of what the performance phase concerns). Minor imprecision in exactly which sub-list item maps to the supplied quote, but not a substantive misreading or overreach.

confirmed.claim: A peak in climbing performance can typically be sustained for about a month, during which an athlete may get two or three really hard sends.
confirmed.quote: Usually when I get someone up to a peak, we can hang out there for about a month and sometimes you'll get two or three really hard sends.
confirmed.evidence:
The claim is a close, faithful paraphrase of the quote: "usually...we can hang out there for about a month" → "can typically be sustained for about a month"; "sometimes you'll get two or three really hard sends" → "may get two or three really hard sends." Both "usually/typically" and "sometimes/may" are comparable hedges — the claim doesn't upgrade an anecdote into an absolute universal law, nor does it claim scientific consensus; it reads as generalized coaching experience, matching the register of the source.

Substantively, it's plausible within mainstream climbing-training knowledge. Peaking/tapering frameworks (Lattice, Horst, Anderson RCTM) describe a limited window of peak readiness after a taper, typically lasting roughly 1–4 weeks before fitness/freshness decays or motivation/skin/weather windows close — "about a month" sits comfortably in that range, especially for outdoor redpoint/onsight sends where opportunistic attempts (weather, conditions, partners) cluster during a good-fitness window. "Two or three really hard sends" during such a window is a modest, believable number for an intermediate-to-advanced climber projecting near their limit, not an inflated or physiologically implausible claim. Nothing here contradicts established periodization consensus; it's consistent with the general (if loosely quantified) idea that peak form is a finite, and not indefinitely extendable, window.

confirmed.claim: During in-season performance phases, a full rest day should be placed before every outside climbing day, and sessions are lower-volume to keep power/strength topped out for bouldering or crux-based routes.
confirmed.quote: Full rest days should be placed before every outside climbing day during in-season performance phases... His in-season performance programs feature lower-volume sessions and are specifically aimed at keeping power and strength topped out for bouldering or crux-based route climbing.
confirmed.evidence: The claim is a faithful, near-verbatim restatement of its own supporting quote — it doesn't add unsupported specifics (no invented percentages, rep schemes, or days-per-week) beyond what the quote states. Substantively, it's consistent with mainstream tapering/peaking principles in climbing and strength sports: reducing session volume while preserving intensity/power output during a performance phase, and building in extra recovery (a full rest day) before the highest-stakes climbing days, is standard practice (echoed even in this repo's own ADR-0003: "deload = volume cut, intensity held"). Recommending a full rest day before an outdoor attempt during peak season is a common, defensible protocol given CNS/power recovery timelines for max-intensity bouldering/route efforts — not an implausible number or a fringe claim. The quote itself frames the volume/intensity structure as "his" (a specific coach's) program, and the claim doesn't strip that attribution away or generalize it into a universal law beyond what's quoted — the first clause ("should be placed") mirrors the source's own phrasing, not an added overreach. Overall this reads as a specific, plausible peaking/tapering protocol rather than a fabricated or contradicted claim.

confirmed.claim: Both max hangs and repeaters are typically performed on an 18-20mm edge.
confirmed.quote: Edge 18-20mm typical.
confirmed.evidence: The claim faithfully restates its source quote ("Edge 18-20mm typical") and uses appropriately hedged language ("typically"), not asserting a rigid universal law. It's also plausible against mainstream hangboard practice: 18-20mm is widely used as a standard reference edge depth by both Lattice-style and Anderson-brothers-style protocols for max hangs and repeaters alike, with intensity typically modulated via added/removed weight rather than changing edge depth — so using the same edge range for both hang types is a common, reasonable convention rather than a fringe claim. There is some legitimate variability across coaches (e.g., some prescribe a larger, easier edge for high-rep repeaters and a smaller edge for max-intensity hangs, or use smaller edges like 6-10mm for advanced max-hang testing), so "typically" is doing real work here and the claim shouldn't be read as an absolute rule — but as a hedged, source-faithful generalization it doesn't overreach or contradict consensus.
